title: Who Is Jesus?
subtitle: Isa ibn Maryam in Islam and Christ in Christianity — two answers to history's most important question
summary: Jesus of Nazareth — called Isa ibn Maryam (Jesus, son of Mary) in the Quran — is deeply honored by both Islam and Christianity, yet the two faiths answer the question 'Who is he?' very differently. Islam teaches that Jesus (peace be upon him) was born of the Virgin Mary by a miracle, that he is the Messiah and one of God's greatest messengers, and that he performed miracles by God's permission — but that he is neither God nor the son of God. Mainstream Christianity teaches that Jesus is the second person of the Trinity: the eternal Word of God who became human, was crucified for humanity's sins, and rose again. This article presents both positions from their own sources and then compares them against the quoted texts themselves.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Jesus of Nazareth is loved and honored by more people than any other figure in history — by roughly two billion Christians and roughly two billion Muslims. But the two faiths give different answers to the question of who he actually is.

Islam teaches that Jesus — known in Arabic as Isa ibn Maryam, 'Jesus, son of Mary' (peace be upon him) — was born of the Virgin Mary by a miracle, that he is the Messiah promised to Israel, a mighty messenger of God who performed miracles by God's permission, and a man who will be honored in this life and the next. Islam also teaches, just as firmly, that he is not God and not the son of God.

Mainstream Christianity teaches that Jesus is the second person of the Trinity — one God existing in three persons — the eternal Word of God who took on human nature, was crucified for humanity's sins, and rose from the dead.

This article presents each position as its own scholars state it, then places the quoted texts side by side so you can weigh them for yourself.

sections.title: Introduction: A Question Both Faiths Care About
sections.body:
No conversation between a Muslim and a Christian gets very far before it arrives at Jesus. That is not an accident. For Christians, Jesus is the center of the faith itself. For Muslims, belief in Jesus is not optional either: the Quran names him dozens of times, devotes an entire chapter to his mother Mary (Surah Maryam, the nineteenth chapter of the Quran), and makes belief in him one of the required articles of faith. A person cannot be a Muslim while rejecting Jesus.

This surprises many readers meeting Islam for the first time. Islam is, in a real sense, a Jesus-affirming religion. It affirms his miraculous virgin birth, his title of Messiah (al-Masih in Arabic — the anointed one promised to the Children of Israel), his miracles, and his honored rank among the greatest messengers God ever sent.

Where the two faiths part ways is on one precise point: is Jesus divine? Christianity's mainstream answer, settled in its creeds, is yes — he is God the Son, fully divine and fully human. Islam's answer is no — he is God's servant and messenger, fully human and nothing less than magnificent in that role.

Because the disagreement is precise, it can be examined honestly. In what follows, every verse of the Quran and the Bible is quoted directly from its source text, so that nothing rests on anyone's memory or paraphrase.

sections.title: The Real Question: Not Whether Jesus Matters, but Who He Is
sections.body:
It helps to clear away what is not in dispute. Islam and Christianity agree that Jesus existed, that he was born of the Virgin Mary without a human father, that he is the Messiah, that he performed genuine miracles, that he taught people to worship God, and that his story is not over — both traditions expect his return before the end of history.

That is a remarkable amount of common ground. The disagreement sits on top of it, and it can be stated in one sentence. Christianity says Jesus is the Creator who entered His creation; Islam says Jesus is the greatest of a long line of human messengers sent by the Creator.

Everything else — the Trinity, the crucifixion as atonement for sin, the meaning of the title 'Son of God' — flows from that single fork in the road.

So the fair way to proceed is to ask three questions in order. What does the Quran say Jesus is? What does the Bible say Jesus is, as mainstream Christianity reads it? And when the two sets of texts are placed side by side, which portrait do they most naturally support? The sections below take those questions one at a time.

sections.title: Historical Context: From Roman Judea to the Creeds
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century, in Judea and Galilee, lands then under Roman rule. The Judaism of his time is known to historians as Second Temple Judaism — the period when the rebuilt Temple still stood in Jerusalem — and its heartbeat was uncompromising monotheism. The daily Jewish confession, the Shema, declared that the Lord is one, and Jesus himself is recorded in Mark's Gospel reciting exactly that confession (we will quote it below). Whatever else the first hearers of Jesus understood him to be, they heard him inside that world: a Jewish teacher, preaching to Jews, about the one God of Israel.

After Jesus' time on earth ended, his followers spread across the Roman world, and a long debate began over how to describe him. Historians of doctrine document that the precise formulations Christians now recite took shape gradually over the first several centuries, as church thinkers with differing views — some holding Jesus to be subordinate to the Father, others insisting on his full deity — argued their cases.

A decisive moment came in the year 325, when the emperor Constantine hosted a council of bishops at Nicaea. The council produced the original Nicene Creed, declaring that the Father and the Son are homoousios — Greek for 'one in essence' or 'consubstantial' — and it condemned the position of Arius, a priest who taught that the Son was a created being. Later councils, including those of 431 and 451, continued to refine the doctrine, and classical Christian teaching came to affirm that Christ is one person with both a divine and a human nature. (The dates and outcomes above are confirmed by the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's history of Trinitarian doctrine.)

Christians understand this history as the church clarifying, under God's guidance, what was implicit in Jesus' words all along. Muslims read the same history differently: as evidence that the doctrine of Jesus' deity was a later development, moving step by step away from the simple monotheism Jesus preached in Galilee. The historical facts are shared; the interpretation is the debate.

sections.title: Jesus in the Quran: Servant, Messiah, Miracle
sections.body:
The Quran's account of Jesus begins with an angelic announcement to Mary:

إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ يَٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍۢ مِّنْهُ ٱسْمُهُ ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ وَجِيهًۭا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْءَاخِرَةِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ

"[And mention] when the angels said, 'O Mary, indeed Allah gives you good tidings of a word from Him, whose name will be the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary - distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near [to Allah]." (Quran 3:45)

Notice the titles packed into one verse: Messiah, son of Mary, honored in both worlds. The announcement continues:

وَيُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًۭا وَمِنَ ٱلصَّٰلِحِينَ

"He will speak to the people in the cradle and in maturity and will be of the righteous.'" (Quran 3:46)

Mary, unmarried and chaste, asks the obvious question — and receives the Quran's explanation of the virgin birth:

قَالَتْ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى وَلَدٌۭ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌۭ ۖ قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًۭا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

"She said, 'My Lord, how will I have a child when no man has touched me?' [The angel] said, 'Such is Allah; He creates what He wills. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, "Be," and it is." (Quran 3:47)

The virgin birth, in the Quran, is a creative miracle — like the creation of Adam, who had neither father nor mother — not evidence of divine sonship. God says 'Be,' and it is.

In Surah Maryam, the infant Jesus speaks from the cradle, and his very first recorded words define him:

قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا

"[Jesus] said, 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet." (Quran 19:30)

وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّۭا

"And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive" (Quran 19:31)

وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَتِى وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِى جَبَّارًۭا شَقِيًّۭا

"And [made me] dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a wretched tyrant." (Quran 19:32)

وَٱلسَّلَٰمُ عَلَىَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدتُّ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّۭا

"And peace is on me the day I was born and the day I will die and the day I am raised alive.'" (Quran 19:33)

Servant of God, prophet, blessed, a man of prayer and charity, devoted to his mother: this is the Quran's Jesus in his own miraculous words.

The Quran also affirms his miracles and divine support:

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَقَفَّيْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ بِٱلرُّسُلِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ وَأَيَّدْنَٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ ۗ أَفَكُلَّمَا جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُكُمُ ٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ فَفَرِيقًۭا كَذَّبْتُمْ وَفَرِيقًۭا تَقْتُلُونَ

"And We did certainly give Moses the Torah and followed up after him with messengers. And We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Pure Spirit. But is it [not] that every time a messenger came to you, [O Children of Israel], with what your souls did not desire, you were arrogant? And a party [of messengers] you denied and another party you killed." (Quran 2:87)

Jesus stands here in a chain of messengers reaching back through Moses. And the Quran states his human nature with disarming simplicity:

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌۭ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌۭ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

"The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded." (Quran 5:75)

'They both used to eat food' — a quiet, powerful argument. One who eats is a creature with needs; the Creator needs nothing.

Finally, the Quran addresses Christians directly, with respect but without ambiguity:

يَٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌۭ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًۭا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۖ سُبْحَٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًۭا

"O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, 'Three'; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs." (Quran 4:171)

The verse honors Jesus with three titles — messenger of God, His word, a soul from Him — and in the same breath draws the line: do not commit excess; God is one; He has no son.

sections.title: Jesus in the Bible: The Christian Reading and the Texts
sections.body:
Fairness requires stating the Christian position as Christians themselves state it. Mainstream Christianity — Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant alike — teaches that Jesus is the eternal Word of God who took on human nature: one person, fully divine and fully human. This is monotheism as Christians define it: not three gods, but one God existing in three persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. In support, Christian scholars point especially to the opening of John's Gospel, which describes God's eternal Word taking on flesh, to passages where Jesus forgives sins and accepts worship, and to the early church's practice of praying to Christ. On the classical Christian reading, Jesus' human limitations belong to his human nature, while his divine nature remained fully God.

That is the doctrine. Now let us hear the texts themselves, quoted from the World English Bible translation. In John's Gospel, Jesus prays to the Father the night before his arrest:

"This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ." (John 17:3)

Here Jesus calls the Father 'the only true God' and describes himself as the one 'whom you sent' — the very definition of a messenger.

When asked which commandment is greatest, Jesus answers with Israel's ancient confession of God's oneness:

"Jesus answered, 'The greatest is, "Hear, Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one:"'" (Mark 12:29)

After the resurrection accounts, John records Jesus saying to Mary Magdalene:

"Jesus said to her, 'Don't hold me, for I haven't yet ascended to my Father; but go to my brothers, and tell them, "I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God."'" (John 20:17)

Jesus speaks of 'my God' — he has a God whom he worships, the same God his followers worship.

When a man addresses him as 'Good teacher,' Jesus deflects the compliment toward God:

"Behold, one came to him and said, 'Good teacher, what good thing shall I do, that I may have eternal life?' He said to him, 'Why do you call me good? No one is good but one, that is, God. But if you want to enter into life, keep the commandments.'" (Matthew 19:16-17)

Jesus also describes the source of his authority in terms of dependence and obedience:

"I can of myself do nothing. As I hear, I judge, and my judgment is righteous; because I don't seek my own will, but the will of my Father who sent me." (John 5:30)

In the Acts of the Apostles, Peter — Jesus' chief disciple — introduces Jesus to a Jerusalem crowd in these words:

"'Men of Israel, hear these words! Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved by God to you by mighty works and wonders and signs which God did by him among you, even as you yourselves know,'" (Acts 2:22)

A man approved by God, whose miracles were things 'God did by him' — this is how the earliest recorded sermon describes Jesus.

And in a letter attributed to Paul, the distinction between God and Jesus is stated as a formula:

"For there is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus," (1 Timothy 2:5)

Christian interpreters have readings of each of these passages — most commonly, that Jesus speaks here from his human nature, or in his role as the sent one, without denying his divine nature. That interpretive framework is genuinely held and should be represented honestly. The question the reader must weigh is whether that framework arises from these texts or is brought to them.

sections.title: Side by Side: The Two Portraits and the Texts
sections.body:
Islam holds that Jesus is the Messiah, born of a virgin, a worker of miracles by God's permission, one of the five greatest messengers, honored in this world and the next — and a created human servant of the one God, not God incarnate and not God's literal son.

Mainstream Christianity holds that Jesus is the second person of the Trinity: the eternal Word, consubstantial with the Father (one in essence, as the Nicene Creed puts it), who became man, died on the cross as an atoning sacrifice, and rose again. Christianity insists this is monotheism: one divine being, three persons.

The texts say, in the passages quoted above: that Jesus called the Father 'the only true God' and himself the one sent (John 17:3); that he recited Israel's confession that 'the Lord is one' (Mark 12:29); that he spoke of 'my God and your God' (John 20:17); that he redirected the word 'good' to God alone (Matthew 19:16-17); that he could do nothing of himself and sought only the will of the One who sent him (John 5:30); that his chief disciple called him 'a man approved by God' through works 'God did by him' (Acts 2:22); and that an early Christian letter calls him 'the man Christ Jesus,' the mediator between the one God and mankind (1 Timothy 2:5). The Quran, for its part, says he is God's servant, prophet, Messiah, word, and messenger — and explicitly not a son of God (Quran 19:30, 4:171).

Here is the striking observation: every one of those Bible verses could be printed inside a Quranic account of Jesus without friction. The Quranic portrait — a human Messiah, empowered by God, calling people to worship the one God — matches the plain sense of these Gospel sayings. The classical Christian portrait can absorb these verses only with the help of the two-natures framework developed in the centuries after Jesus, and formalized at councils such as Nicaea in 325.

A fair-minded reader should know that Christians also cite verses they read as pointing to Jesus' divinity, chiefly in John's Gospel, and this library examines those passages in the related articles. But on the sayings quoted here — sayings from Jesus' own recorded prayers and his disciples' earliest preaching — the simpler explanation, Muslims argue, is the Quran's.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
Misconception one: 'Muslims reject Jesus.' The opposite is true. Belief in Jesus as the Messiah and a true messenger of God is a required article of Islamic faith. The Quran mentions Jesus by name far more often than it mentions Muhammad (peace be upon them both), and Muslims traditionally add 'peace be upon him' whenever they say his name. What Islam rejects is not Jesus but his deification.

Misconception two: 'Christians worship three gods.' Mainstream Christian theology explicitly denies this. The doctrine of the Trinity, as Christian scholarship defines it, is that one God exists in three persons — a form of monotheism on its own definition. The Quran's command 'do not say, "Three"' (Quran 4:171) is understood by Muslims as a warning against any formulation that divides God's absolute oneness, however it is labeled; but honest dialogue requires describing the Trinity as Christians actually teach it, not as a crude tritheism.

Misconception three: 'The Quran calls Jesus a "word from God," so it secretly affirms his divinity.' In the Quran's own explanation, Jesus is a 'word' because he came into being by God's creative command — 'He only says to it, "Be," and it is' (Quran 3:47). The title marks the miracle of his conception, not a share in God's essence.

Misconception four: 'Islam denies the virgin birth and the miracles.' It affirms both, emphatically. Jesus' virgin birth (Quran 3:45-47) and his 'clear proofs' — his miracles, performed with God's support (Quran 2:87) — are Quranic doctrine. Notably, Acts 2:22 frames the miracles the same way: works 'which God did by him.'

Misconception five: 'Denying Jesus' divinity means disrespecting him.' In the Islamic view, calling Jesus God's faithful servant is not a demotion; servanthood to God is the highest honor a creature can hold, and Jesus himself is quoted opening his mission with exactly that title: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah' (Quran 19:30).

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Islam's answer to 'Who is Jesus?' is confident because it is simple, and simple because, Muslims believe, it is what Jesus himself said. He is the Messiah, son of Mary: conceived by a miracle, strengthened by the Pure Spirit, sent to the Children of Israel with clear proofs, a man of prayer, charity, and tenderness toward his mother, upon whom is peace the day he was born, the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive.

The Quran does not lower Jesus; it locates him — at the summit of humanity, not on the throne of God. And it argues that this is exactly where the Jesus of the Gospels places himself: praying to 'the only true God,' confessing that 'the Lord is one,' calling the Father 'my God and your God,' doing nothing of himself.

To the Christian reader, Islam extends a claim worth pondering rather than an insult: that in honoring Jesus as God's servant and Messiah, Muslims are not subtracting from his glory but restoring his own testimony. To the seeker, the invitation is even simpler. Read the verses again — all of them quoted above from their sources — and ask which portrait requires the fewest added assumptions.

The Quran's final word on the matter is both a warning and a mercy: 'do not commit excess in your religion... Indeed, Allah is but one God' (Quran 4:171). Loving Jesus rightly, Islam teaches, means worshiping the God Jesus worshiped.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims believe in Jesus?
A: Yes — belief in Jesus is mandatory in Islam. Muslims believe he is the Messiah, born of the Virgin Mary, a mighty messenger of God who performed miracles by God's permission. Rejecting Jesus would place a person outside Islam.

Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus is God or the son of God?
A: No. The Quran teaches that Jesus is God's servant and messenger, fully human, and explicitly states that God is exalted above having a son (Quran 4:171). Islam sees the deification of Jesus as the kind of 'excess in religion' the Quran warns against.

Q: Does Islam accept the virgin birth?
A: Yes. The Quran narrates the annunciation to Mary and her question 'How will I have a child when no man has touched me?' — answered by God's creative command, 'Be,' and it is (Quran 3:47). The virgin birth is a miracle of creation, not proof of divine sonship.

Q: What does 'Messiah' mean in Islam?
A: Al-Masih (the Messiah) is a title of honor the Quran gives uniquely to Jesus, marking him as the anointed one promised to the Children of Israel. Unlike in Christian theology, the title does not imply divinity; the Quran pairs it with 'son of Mary' and 'messenger of Allah' (Quran 4:171, 5:75).

Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus was crucified?
A: Mainstream Islamic teaching, based on the Quran, holds that Jesus was not killed by crucifixion but that God saved him and raised him to Himself, and that he will return before the end of time. That subject deserves its own careful treatment and is covered in related articles in this library.

Q: If Christians and Muslims both love Jesus, why does the difference matter?
A: Because both faiths agree that who God is — and who may be worshiped — is the most important question a human being faces. If Jesus is God, refusing him worship is a grave error; if he is God's human messenger, directing worship to him is precisely the excess both the Quran and, Muslims argue, Jesus' own recorded words warn against.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following works are offered as general further reading for those who want to go deeper — from Muslim, Christian, and academic perspectives. They are listed as starting points, not as proof of any specific claim made above; every scriptural quotation in this article was drawn directly from the source texts cited in each verse's attribution, and the historical dates concerning the early councils were checked against the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's treatment of the history of Trinitarian doctrine.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature. Bart D. Ehrman — How Jesus Became God. Dale Tuggy — 'Trinity,' Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy. C.S. Lewis — Mere Christianity. W. Montgomery Watt — Muslim-Christian Encounters: Perceptions and Misperceptions.

relatedArticles.title: Did Jesus Worship God?
relatedArticles.subtitle: What the Gospel accounts and the Quran both record about the prayer life of Jesus — and what it means
relatedArticles.summary: Both the Bible and the Quran record Jesus praying, fasting, and prostrating to God. The Gospels show him praying before dawn, all night on a mountainside, and face-down on the ground in Gethsemane, while the Quran quotes him saying 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah.' Mainstream Christianity understands this prayer as the incarnate Son communing with the Father within the Trinity; Islam understands it as the devotion of God's honored prophet and servant. This article quotes the key texts from both scriptures, presents both readings fairly, and then makes the Islamic case from the texts themselves.

sections.title: In a Nutshell
sections.body:
The four Gospels repeatedly describe Jesus praying: alone before dawn, all night on a mountainside, and face-down on the ground in the garden of Gethsemane on the night of his arrest. He fasted, he prostrated, and he taught that God alone deserves worship. The Quran describes the same man in one sentence: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah.'

This article gathers the actual texts — five passages from the Quran and seven from the Bible, each quoted word for word — and lets them speak. It then explains, fairly, how Christians and Muslims each understand a praying Jesus, and why Islam sees in his prayer life the simplest answer to the question of who he really was: not God receiving worship, but God's beloved prophet offering it.

sections.title: A Question Both Faiths Can Answer Together
sections.body:
Ask a Muslim and a Christian who Jesus is, and you will get two very different answers. Ask them whether Jesus prayed, and you will get the same answer from both. The Gospel writers mention his prayer life so often, and so casually, that it reads less like a claim being argued and more like a habit everyone around him knew: he slipped away before sunrise to pray, he spent whole nights in prayer, and in his final hours he fell on his face and pleaded with God.

The Quran describes the same pattern. In it, Jesus — called Isa in Arabic — introduces himself with the words 'I am the servant of Allah' and says that God 'has enjoined upon me prayer' for as long as he lives.

That agreement makes this question unusually valuable for a sincere seeker. Many debates about Jesus turn on disputed verses, Greek grammar, or complicated manuscript history. This one does not. The evidence sits on the surface of both scriptures, in plain narrative sentences. Our task is simply to gather those sentences and then ask, with fairness to both faiths, what a praying, fasting, prostrating Jesus tells us about who he was.

sections.title: What Exactly Are We Asking?
sections.body:
First, a definition. In both the Bible and the Quran, worship means devotion directed to God: prayer, fasting, prostration (bowing with the face to the ground), thanksgiving, and obedience offered to the Creator. The Arabic word is ibadah, and it comes from the same root as abd, meaning 'servant.' A worshiper is, by definition, a servant of the One being worshiped.

So the question 'Did Jesus worship God?' really contains two questions. The first is factual: do the records show Jesus performing acts of worship, and if so, toward whom did he direct them? The second is interpretive: if Jesus did worship God, what does that tell us about his identity? Worship flows in one direction — from the dependent to the One depended upon, from creature to Creator. A person who prays is asking; a person who fasts is submitting; a person who prostrates places himself low before another.

As we will see, Muslims and Christians largely agree on the factual answer. Where they part ways is on the second question — and that difference goes to the heart of what separates the two faiths.

sections.title: A Praying Jew in First-Century Palestine
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century in Roman-ruled Palestine, and he lived as a Jew. The everyday piety of a devout Jew in that world included regular prayer, fasting, worship at the synagogue and the Jerusalem Temple, and the declaration that God is one. The Gospels — Christianity's own foundational documents — show Jesus fully inside this world: he attends synagogue, keeps the festivals, fasts, and constantly quotes the Hebrew scriptures, including their commands to worship God alone.

It is also historically important that the formal doctrines about Jesus' divine nature were articulated after his lifetime, as Christianity spread through the Greek and Roman world. The Council of Nicaea, which met in 325 CE, declared the Son to be of the same substance as the Father, and a later council in the mid-fifth century, at Chalcedon, defined Jesus as one person with two complete natures, fully divine and fully human. Christians regard these councils as clarifying what the scriptures already taught. Muslims regard them as adding a layer of philosophy on top of a simpler original message.

Either way, one point matters for this article: the reports of Jesus praying are older than the councils that explained why he prayed. The prayer came first; the theology about the prayer came later. That order is worth keeping in mind as we turn to the texts themselves.

sections.title: Jesus in the Quran: The Servant Who Prays
sections.body:
The Quran mentions Jesus in dozens of verses and consistently presents him through one lens: he is God's honored Messiah, messenger, and servant, and his message was that God alone deserves worship. His very first words in the Quran — spoken miraculously as an infant in the cradle to defend his mother Mary — are a declaration of servanthood:

قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا

'[Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet.' (Quran 19:30, Saheeh International)

The Arabic phrase abd Allah means 'servant of Allah.' In Islam this is not a lowly title but the highest one a human being can hold. The infant Jesus continues:

وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّۭا

'And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive' (Quran 19:31, Saheeh International)

Notice what Jesus says God commanded him to do: salah (formal prayer) and zakah (obligatory charity) — the same core duties every Muslim carries. In the Quran's portrait, Jesus is not the object of worship; he is a worshiper, under God's command, for as long as he lives.

His message to others matched his own practice. Speaking to the Children of Israel, he says:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۗ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

'Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path."' (Quran 3:51, Saheeh International)

The Quran repeats this sentence, in almost identical words, as the summary of his teaching:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

'Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is a straight path."' (Quran 43:64, Saheeh International)

Twice, the same claim: God is 'my Lord and your Lord.' Jesus places himself on the same side of the Creator-creature line as his listeners. Finally, the Quran gives Jesus a speech on the Day of Judgment, when God asks him whether he ever told people to take him and his mother as gods besides Allah. Jesus answers:

مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَمَرْتَنِى بِهِۦٓ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۚ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِى كُنتَ أَنتَ ٱلرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ شَهِيدٌ

'I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness.' (Quran 5:117, Saheeh International)

For the Quran, then, the answer to our question is not a side detail. Jesus worshiping God, and calling everyone else to do the same, is the whole point of who Jesus is.

sections.title: Jesus in the Gospels: A Life Full of Prayer
sections.body:
Now the Bible, quoted here from the World English Bible translation. What is striking is how strongly the Gospel narrative agrees with the Quranic portrait on the factual question. Mark, widely regarded by scholars as the earliest Gospel, describes Jesus' habit this way:

Early in the morning, while it was still dark, he rose up and went out, and departed into a deserted place, and prayed there. (Mark 1:35)

Luke reports the same pattern as routine:

But he withdrew himself into the desert, and prayed. (Luke 5:16)

These were not public performances. The texts stress that he prayed alone, in deserted places, before anyone was awake. Before choosing his twelve closest disciples, Luke tells us:

In these days, he went out to the mountain to pray, and he continued all night in prayer to God. (Luke 6:12)

Note Luke's own wording: all night in prayer 'to God.' The most intense scene comes on the night of his arrest, in the garden of Gethsemane:

He went forward a little, fell on his face, and prayed, saying, “My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass away from me; nevertheless, not what I desire, but what you desire.” (Matthew 26:39)

Here is prostration — falling on the face — the very posture Muslims take in every salah. And here is surrender: my desire yields to Yours. The Arabic word for that surrender of the will to God is islam.

The Letter to the Hebrews, reflecting back on Jesus' earthly life, sums it up in one remarkable sentence:

He, in the days of his flesh, having offered up prayers and petitions with strong crying and tears to him who was able to save him from death, and having been heard for his godly fear, (Hebrews 5:7)

Jesus offers prayers and petitions; God is the one 'able to save him'; and he is heard because of his reverence toward God.

In John's Gospel — the Gospel with the loftiest language about Jesus — Jesus prays aloud before his disciples:

Jesus said these things, and lifting up his eyes to heaven, he said, “Father, the time has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may also glorify you; even as you gave him authority over all flesh, so he will give eternal life to all whom you have given him. This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ. (John 17:1-3)

In his own prayer, Jesus calls the Father 'the only true God' and describes himself as the one 'whom you sent.' Finally, when the devil tempts him, Jesus states the rule that governs all worship:

Then Jesus said to him, “Get behind me, Satan! For it is written, ‘You shall worship the Lord your God, and you shall serve him only.’” (Matthew 4:10)

He is quoting the Torah, the scripture of his people: worship belongs to God alone.

sections.title: Two Readings of One Record
sections.body:
Islam holds that Jesus worshiped God because he was exactly what he said he was: God's servant and messenger. His prayer, fasting, and prostration were the natural acts of a created being before his Creator, and his instruction 'so worship Him' places his followers on the same path he walked. On this reading, nothing in his prayer life needs explaining; it is simply what a prophet does.

Mainstream Christianity holds — and fairness requires stating this properly — that Jesus is the eternal Son of God who took on a complete human nature. Christians do not believe in three gods; the doctrine of the Trinity teaches one God in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. When Jesus prays, Christian theology says, the incarnate Son prays in and through his human nature, and his prayer expresses the eternal loving communion between the Son and the Father. On this reading, Jesus praying to the Father is not one god petitioning another, and not God talking to himself, but a real relationship between distinct persons within the one God.

The texts say: Jesus rose before dawn to pray, withdrew habitually to pray, prayed all night 'to God,' fell on his face and yielded his will to the Father's, offered petitions 'with strong crying and tears to him who was able to save him from death,' was 'heard for his godly fear,' addressed the Father as 'the only true God,' and taught that God alone is to be worshiped and served. The Quran says he called himself the servant of Allah, was commanded to pray and give charity for as long as he lived, and told everyone that God is 'my Lord and your Lord.'

The Islamic argument at this point is an argument from simplicity. Both readings are internally coherent. But one requires interpretive machinery the texts themselves never state — two natures in one person, prayer offered by one divine person to another — while the other requires nothing beyond the plain sentences. Read at face value, the man these passages describe is a worshiper of God. That is the entire Islamic claim, and it is the Quran's claim in Jesus' own voice.

sections.title: Clearing Up Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
Misconception one: 'Christians believe Jesus prayed to himself.' This is not what Christian theology teaches, and repeating it attacks a position no informed Christian holds. Trinitarian doctrine distinguishes the persons of the Father and the Son, so in Christian terms the Son prays to the Father, not to himself. The serious Islamic critique is not that the doctrine is self-evidently absurd, but that it is a framework added onto texts that read naturally without it.

Misconception two: 'Muslims deny or diminish Jesus.' The opposite is true. The Quran calls Jesus the Messiah, a word from God, strengthened by the Holy Spirit, born of a virgin, and a worker of miracles by God's permission. A Muslim's faith is not valid without believing in him. What Islam denies is one thing only: that he is God or the literal son of God.

Misconception three: 'Jesus prayed only as an example for others, not because he needed to.' The texts resist this. Mark 1:35 and Luke 5:16 describe private prayer, away from every observer, before dawn and in deserted places — the opposite of a teaching demonstration. And Hebrews 5:7 describes petitions offered 'with strong crying and tears' to the One able to save him. That is real need, not theater.

Misconception four: 'Calling Jesus a servant of God is an insult.' In the Bible's own vocabulary, 'servant of God' is a title of honor carried by the prophets, and the Quran uses the equivalent word, abd, for its most honored figures, including the Prophet Muhammad himself. When the Quran's infant Jesus says 'I am the servant of Allah,' he is claiming a rank, not accepting a demotion.

sections.title: The Straight Path of the Praying Messiah
sections.body:
Stand back from the details and look at the shape of the life the sources describe. Here is a man who rises in the dark to pray while his companions sleep. He fasts. He withdraws to deserts and mountains to be alone with God. He spends entire nights in prayer. On the worst night of his life he does not proclaim his own power; he falls on his face in the dust and says, in effect: Your will, not mine. He calls the Father 'the only true God.' He answers the devil with scripture: worship God, serve him only.

Islam asks one simple question: whom does that life describe — the One who is worshiped, or one who worships?

The Quran's answer is that this is the life of a beloved servant and mighty messenger of God: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah.' Nothing in the Gospel record of his devotion has to be reinterpreted or qualified for that answer to work. And Jesus' own recorded instruction — worship the Lord your God and serve him only; Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him — is precisely what a Muslim does five times a day, with the same prostration Jesus made in Gethsemane.

To honor Jesus, on the Islamic view, is not to worship him but to worship as he worshiped and whom he worshiped. That, the Quran says twice in his own voice, is 'a straight path' — the sirat al-mustaqim from which this library takes its name. The invitation of Islam is simply this: follow the praying Messiah to the God he prayed to.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: If Jesus is God, why did he pray?
A: Christians answer that the eternal Son took on a real human nature, and in that nature he genuinely prayed, depended, and submitted; his prayer also expresses the eternal relationship between the Son and the Father. Muslims answer that a far simpler explanation is the one Jesus gives in the Quran and lives out in the Gospels: he prayed because God was his God and Lord.

Q: Do Christians believe Jesus prayed to himself?
A: No. Trinitarian teaching distinguishes the persons of the Father and the Son within the one God, so the Son prays to the Father. Muslims find this framework unnecessary, but it should be described accurately.

Q: Did Jesus pray the way Muslims pray?
A: There are striking overlaps: Matthew 26:39 shows him prostrate with his face to the ground, he fasted, and he prayed through the night and before dawn. Islam does not claim his ritual was identical to today's salah, only that his posture before God — servant before Lord — is the same.

Q: Doesn't John 17 support the Trinity, since Jesus asks the Father to glorify the Son?
A: Christians do read it that way, and that reading should be engaged rather than dismissed. Muslims reply that in the very same prayer Jesus calls the Father 'the only true God' and defines himself as the one God 'sent' — the vocabulary of a messenger, which is exactly the Quran's category for him.

Q: Is 'servant of God' a demotion of Jesus?
A: Not in either scripture's vocabulary. The Bible gives the title to its greatest prophets, and in the Quran servanthood to God is the highest human station, applied to Muhammad as well. Jesus claiming it from the cradle is presented as an honor, not an insult.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below are offered for general further reading only. They are not cited as proof of any specific claim in this article; the argument above rests on the scripture passages quoted, which were retrieved word for word from the Quran (Saheeh International translation) and the World English Bible.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature. A scholarly collection showing how deeply Jesus is loved and honored in the Islamic tradition.

Bart D. Ehrman — How Jesus Became God. A historical study by a non-Muslim scholar of how beliefs about Jesus' divinity developed in early Christianity.

C. S. Lewis — Mere Christianity. A classic, accessible presentation of mainstream Christian belief, useful for understanding the Christian position in its own voice.

Muhammad 'Ata ur-Rahim — Jesus, Prophet of Islam. An Islamic perspective on the history of beliefs about Jesus.

relatedArticles.title: Who Follows Jesus More Closely?
relatedArticles.subtitle: The recorded habits of Jesus — prostration, fasting, the greeting of peace — and where they live on today
relatedArticles.summary: The Gospels record Jesus praying with his face to the ground, fasting, giving secret charity, greeting with peace, keeping the food laws, and teaching that only those who do God's will truly follow him. Muslims today keep strikingly similar practices as daily obligations, while mainstream Christianity understands following Jesus as faith in his person rather than replication of his customs. This article quotes the Quran and the Bible verbatim on each practice, presents the Christian understanding fairly, and then makes the Islamic case from the texts themselves.

sections.title: In a Nutshell
sections.body:
Open the Gospels and watch how Jesus lived from day to day. He fell with his face to the ground when he prayed. He fasted, and assumed his followers would fast. He greeted his disciples with "Peace be to you." He taught secret giving to the poor, and warned that calling him "Lord" means nothing without doing the will of God.

A practicing Muslim today prostrates with the face to the ground in the five daily prayers, fasts a month every year, greets with "peace be upon you" (as-salamu alaykum), gives a fixed share of wealth to the poor, avoids pork, and says "God willing" (insha'Allah) about tomorrow's plans.

Mainstream Christianity does not deny these habits of Jesus; it teaches that following him means faith in his person, not replication of his customs. This article quotes the texts on both sides, presents the Christian understanding fairly, and then makes the Islamic case.

sections.title: Why Ask This Question?
sections.body:
First, a word about tone. This article is not an attempt to score points against sincere Christians, hundreds of millions of whom love Jesus deeply and order their lives around him. Nothing here mocks that devotion.

Every community that honors Jesus claims to follow him. Rather than argue over the word, this article looks at something checkable: the specific, recorded habits of Jesus in the Gospels, set beside the daily religious lives of Christians and Muslims today. Each faith's position is presented as its own scholarship states it, and every quotation is verbatim — the Quran in the Saheeh International translation, the Bible in the World English Bible.

sections.title: What Does It Mean to Follow Someone?
sections.body:
"Following" a person can mean two things: trusting him — believing what he says and giving him your loyalty — or walking the way he walked: praying as he prayed, fasting as he fasted, greeting as he greeted, eating as he ate.

Mainstream Christianity centers on the first meaning: faith in Jesus as Lord and Savior, and growth into his character. His outward customs, on this view, belonged to the covenant era he came to complete. Islam affirms the second meaning as well, while differing sharply about who Jesus is: Muslims honor him as one of God's greatest messengers — not as God — and find that their daily practice matches his recorded practice to a striking degree. So the question becomes precise: line up the recorded habits of Jesus beside the everyday lives of the two communities, and see.

sections.title: The World Jesus Lived In
sections.body:
Jesus was born, lived, and taught as a Jew, among Jews, in a Jewish homeland under Roman rule. On this, Christian, Jewish, and secular historians agree. The Gospels show him attending synagogue, keeping the festivals of his people, and quoting the Law of Moses and the Hebrew prophets as scripture.

The piety of that world had a recognizable shape: bowing and falling on one's face before God, as the Hebrew Bible repeatedly describes prophets doing; fasting; almsgiving as a core duty; the Torah's food laws, which excluded pork outright; the everyday greeting of peace, shalom; and speech that placed future plans under God's will.

The Gospels never show Jesus stepping outside this pattern: he is never recorded eating what the Torah forbids or abandoning the prayer and fasting of his people. The question is what happened afterward. Christianity came to teach that the ceremonial law was fulfilled in Christ and no longer binding, while the practices themselves continue as daily obligations in the worship of Muslims. The rest of this article documents both halves of that sentence from the sources.

sections.title: The Quran on Jesus and His Followers
sections.body:
The Quran speaks of Jesus — Isa in Arabic — with love and reverence, and holds up his first disciples as a model Muslims are told to imitate:

يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوٓا۟ أَنصَارَ ٱللَّهِ كَمَا قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ لِلْحَوَارِيِّۦنَ مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَـَٔامَنَت طَّآئِفَةٌۭ مِّنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَكَفَرَت طَّآئِفَةٌۭ ۖ فَأَيَّدْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ عَلَىٰ عَدُوِّهِمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ ظَٰهِرِينَ

"O you who have believed, be supporters of Allah, as when Jesus, the son of Mary, said to the disciples, "Who are my supporters for Allah?" The disciples said, "We are supporters of Allah." And a faction of the Children of Israel believed and a faction disbelieved. So We supported those who believed against their enemy, and they became dominant." (Quran 61:14)

The "disciples" translates al-hawariyyun, the Quran's name for Jesus's close companions. An earlier surah records the same scene with the disciples' own description of themselves:

۞ فَلَمَّآ أَحَسَّ عِيسَىٰ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ قَالَ مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ ٱللَّهِ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ

"But when Jesus felt [persistence in] disbelief from them, he said, "Who are my supporters for [the cause of] Allah?" The disciples said, "We are supporters for Allah. We have believed in Allah and testify that we are Muslims [submitting to Him]." (Quran 3:52)

The word translated "Muslims" is muslimun, Arabic for "those who submit" to God — a description before it is anyone's proper name, and the Quran applies it to the followers of Jesus. A third passage repeats it in their own voice:

وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى ٱلْحَوَارِيِّۦنَ أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِى وَبِرَسُولِى قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّنَا مُسْلِمُونَ

"And [remember] when I inspired to the disciples, "Believe in Me and in My messenger Jesus." They said, "We have believed, so bear witness that indeed we are Muslims [in submission to Allah]."" (Quran 5:111)

And what did the way of Jesus involve? In Surah Maryam, the infant Jesus speaks miraculously from the cradle:

وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّۭا

"And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive" (Quran 19:31)

Prayer (salah) and almsgiving (zakah) are exactly what the Quranic Jesus says God enjoined on him for life. The Quran also ties love of God to following His messenger:

قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱللَّهَ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى يُحْبِبْكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌۭ رَّحِيمٌۭ

"Say, [O Muhammad], "If you should love Allah, then follow me, [so] Allah will love you and forgive you your sins. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."" (Quran 3:31)

Even the small habit of saying "God willing" is commanded:

وَلَا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَا۟ىْءٍ إِنِّى فَاعِلٌۭ ذَٰلِكَ غَدًا

"And never say of anything, "Indeed, I will do that tomorrow,"" (Quran 18:23)

إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ إِذَا نَسِيتَ وَقُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّى لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هَٰذَا رَشَدًۭا

"Except [when adding], "If Allah wills." And remember your Lord when you forget [it] and say, "Perhaps my Lord will guide me to what is nearer than this to right conduct."" (Quran 18:24)

This is why a Muslim says insha'Allah before speaking of any plan. Keep the phrase in mind; it reappears, almost word for word, in the New Testament below.

sections.title: The Gospel Record of Jesus's Practice
sections.body:
Now the Gospel record, quoted from the World English Bible. Begin in Gethsemane, at the most intense moment of prayer the Gospels describe:

"He went forward a little, fell on his face, and prayed, saying, “My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass away from me; nevertheless, not what I desire, but what you desire.”" (Matthew 26:39, WEB)

He "fell on his face, and prayed." Anyone who has watched Muslim prayer will recognize the posture: the sujud, the prostration that anchors every cycle of the five daily prayers.

In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus takes it for granted that his followers fast; his instruction is only about sincerity:

"“Moreover when you fast, don’t be like the hypocrites, with sad faces. For they disfigure their faces, that they may be seen by men to be fasting. Most certainly I tell you, they have received their reward. But you, when you fast, anoint your head, and wash your face;" (Matthew 6:16-17, WEB)

"When you fast" — not "if." Moments earlier he gives charity the same treatment:

"But when you do merciful deeds, don’t let your left hand know what your right hand does, so that your merciful deeds may be in secret, then your Father who sees in secret will reward you openly." (Matthew 6:3-4, WEB)

After the resurrection appearances begin, John records Jesus's greeting to his gathered disciples:

"When therefore it was evening, on that day, the first day of the week, and when the doors were locked where the disciples were assembled, for fear of the Jews, Jesus came and stood in the middle, and said to them, “Peace be to you.”" (John 20:19, WEB)

"Peace be to you" was the ordinary greeting of Jesus's world — and is the Muslim world's today: as-salamu alaykum. On the Law of Moses that shaped these habits, the Sermon on the Mount reports him saying:

"“Don’t think that I came to destroy the law or the prophets. I didn’t come to destroy, but to fulfill." (Matthew 5:17, WEB)

Jesus also states his own criterion for real following, preserved in two Gospels:

"Not everyone who says to me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ will enter into the Kingdom of Heaven; but he who does the will of my Father who is in heaven." (Matthew 7:21, WEB)

"“Why do you call me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ and don’t do the things which I say?" (Luke 6:46, WEB)

By his own test, following is a matter of doing. Finally, the Letter of James — attributed by long Christian tradition to the brother of the Lord — teaches the very speech habit the Quran commands:

"For you ought to say, “If the Lord wills, we will both live, and do this or that.”" (James 4:15, WEB)

"If the Lord wills": in Arabic, insha'Allah.

sections.title: Practice by Practice
sections.body:
Set the two columns side by side, practice by practice.

Prayer posture. Jesus "fell on his face, and prayed" (Matthew 26:39). Muslims prostrate with the face to the ground in every prayer; in most Christian worship the posture is standing, sitting, or kneeling upright, with full prostration surviving only in rare moments such as ordination rites.

Fasting. Jesus fasted and said "when you fast" (Matthew 6:16-17). Muslims fast every day of Ramadan as a pillar of the faith. In fairness, fasting never disappeared from Christianity: Orthodox fasting seasons and Catholic Lent remain, though in much modern Protestant practice it is optional.

The greeting of peace. Jesus greeted his disciples with "Peace be to you" (John 20:19). As-salamu alaykum is the standard Islamic greeting, exchanged countless times a day; among Christians the greeting of peace survives chiefly inside the liturgy.

Almsgiving. Jesus commanded secret, sincere giving (Matthew 6:3-4). Islam makes almsgiving a pillar: zakah, a fixed yearly share of wealth owed to the poor. Christian generosity is real and often remarkable; the difference is that Islam retains giving as a precisely defined obligation.

Modesty and dress. Christian art has always depicted the world of Jesus and his mother Mary in flowing garments, with Mary's head covered. Islamic norms of modest dress, including the headscarf, sit inside that same older pattern.

Food. The Torah forbids pork, and the Gospels never show Jesus eating it. Muslims still abstain; most Christians eat it, understanding the food laws to have been set aside after Jesus's ministry.

Speech. James 4:15 counsels "If the Lord wills"; the Quran commands the same (18:23-24). Muslims say insha'Allah as a reflex; most Christians' daily speech has no fixed equivalent.

The claim, stated carefully, is not that Christians do none of these things. It is that in ordinary Muslim life the recorded habits of Jesus persist in close to their original form, as daily obligations rather than seasonal or liturgical echoes.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
Misconception one: "Christians simply ignore Jesus's example." False and unfair. The imitation of Christ is one of the richest themes in Christian history — Thomas a Kempis's classic by that very name has been read for centuries. The mainstream position is not indifference; it is a theological judgment that what must be imitated is Jesus's character, while his ceremonial practices belonged to the Law he fulfilled. Christians read passages such as Mark 7, Acts 10, and the letters of Paul as lifting the old food laws and rituals. On its own terms that position is coherent.

Misconception two: "Muslims copied these practices from Jesus." The Islamic claim is not that Islam was reconstructed from the Gospels, but that one God sent all the prophets with one way of worship — the Hebrew Bible itself describes prophets prostrating, fasting, and giving alms — and that Islam is that way continued. The resemblance is not borrowing; it is family likeness.

Misconception three: "This comparison settles which religion is true." It does not. Practice is one strand of evidence; the deeper questions — who God is, who Jesus is, what he taught about worship — are treated in the related articles.

Misconception four: "The disagreement is about whether Jesus kept these practices." Virtually everyone agrees he kept them. The real disagreement is whether they were meant to end with him. Christianity says the law reached its goal in Christ and its ceremonies lapsed. Islam answers that the way of the prophets was never abolished by God, and notes that Jesus himself is quoted saying he came not to destroy the law but to fulfill it (Matthew 5:17). That is the genuine crux.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Return to Jesus's own criterion, quoted above from two Gospels (Matthew 7:21; Luke 6:46): the Kingdom belongs not to everyone who says "Lord, Lord," but to "he who does the will of my Father." Whatever else following Jesus means, by his own words it cannot mean less than doing.

Islam's case is then simple to state. The man the Gospels describe prayed with his face to the ground, fasted, taught secret charity, greeted with peace, kept the food laws of Moses, and submitted his will entirely to God — "not what I desire, but what you desire" (Matthew 26:39). The community whose ordinary members still do all of these things every day, as obedience to the God Jesus worshipped, is the Muslim community. The Quran presents this as no accident: it calls his disciples muslimun, those who submit, and calls believers to answer as they answered (Quran 61:14).

None of this requires doubting the sincerity of Christian love for Jesus. It suggests something gentler and, for a seeker, more interesting: the way of life Jesus actually lived did not vanish from the earth. A reader who admires the prayer of Gethsemane can find it today — practiced, not only read about — among the people who say of Jesus what his own disciples say in the Quran: "We have believed in Allah and testify that we are Muslims [submitting to Him]" (Quran 3:52).

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Does this article claim that Christians do not really follow Jesus?
A: No. Christians follow Jesus according to their own understanding: faith in his person and imitation of his character. The article compares recorded daily practices and lets readers weigh what the resemblance means.

Q: If Muslims do not worship Jesus, why do they pray the way he prayed?
A: Because Muslims worship the God Jesus prayed to. Islam teaches that all prophets practiced one way of devotion to the one God, which Jesus's recorded practice confirms.

Q: Did Jesus eat pork?
A: The Gospels never record it, and as an observant Jew he kept the Torah's food laws, which forbid pork. Christians who eat pork rely on later passages such as Mark 7 and Acts 10, understood as ending the food laws. Muslims continue the abstention Jesus himself practiced.

Q: Is saying 'God willing' really in the Bible?
A: Yes. James 4:15 reads: "For you ought to say, 'If the Lord wills, we will both live, and do this or that.'" The Quran gives the same command in 18:23-24, and Muslims practice it daily as insha'Allah.

Q: Does this comparison prove Islam is true?
A: By itself, no — and the article says so. It is one strand of evidence; questions about God, the Trinity, and worship are examined in the related articles.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following books span Muslim, Christian, and academic perspectives; listing them is not an endorsement of every argument they contain.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature.

Geza Vermes — Jesus the Jew: A Historian's Reading of the Gospels.

E. P. Sanders — The Historical Figure of Jesus.

Thomas a Kempis — The Imitation of Christ.

C. S. Lewis — Mere Christianity.

relatedArticles.title: What Is the Trinity?
relatedArticles.subtitle: The doctrine as Christian theology defines it, how it was formalized at Nicaea and Constantinople, and the Quran's response
relatedArticles.summary: The Trinity is mainstream Christianity's central doctrine: one God existing eternally as three co-equal, co-eternal persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — sharing one essence. This article presents the doctrine as Christian theology itself defines it and traces its formal definition at the Councils of Nicaea (325 CE) and Constantinople (381 CE). It then reads the Bible passages Christians cite alongside passages that complicate the doctrine, and presents the Quran's response of absolute divine oneness (tawhid). Written for beginners, with every verse quoted from published translations.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Most Christian churches teach that God is a Trinity: one God who exists eternally as three distinct, co-equal, co-eternal persons — the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit — sharing one divine essence. This article explains that doctrine exactly as mainstream Christian theology states it, and traces how it was formally defined at the Councils of Nicaea (325 CE) and Constantinople (381 CE).

It then reads the Bible passages Christians cite alongside passages that complicate the doctrine, and presents the Quran's response: God is absolutely one, without persons, partners, or a son. No prior knowledge of either faith is assumed.

sections.title: Introduction: Defining the Doctrine Fairly
sections.body:
The Trinity is the central doctrine of mainstream Christianity, shared by Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, and most Protestant churches. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes it like this: “A Trinity doctrine is commonly expressed as the statement that the one God exists as or in three equally divine ‘Persons’, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit.”

Three technical terms will help. A “person” (Greek: hypostasis) here does not mean a separate god, but a real, distinct “who” within the one God. “Essence” or “substance” (Greek: ousia) means what God is — the one divine being itself. And homoousios, meaning “of the same essence,” is the word the Council of Nicaea used to declare that the Son is fully God in exactly the same sense that the Father is God: co-equal and co-eternal, not a lesser or later being.

Just as important is what the doctrine does not claim. It does not teach three gods — that idea, called tritheism, has always been condemned as heresy. Nor does it teach that one divine person merely plays three roles — that idea, called modalism, was rejected too. The doctrine holds, as a mystery beyond full human comprehension, that there is exactly one God, and that Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are each fully that one God while remaining really distinct. Any fair comparison with Islam must start from that definition, not from a caricature.

sections.title: The Main Question
sections.body:
The question of this article is simple to state: what exactly does the doctrine of the Trinity claim, where did the formal doctrine come from, and how does it fare when tested against the scriptures that Christians and Muslims each hold up?

For a Christian reader, the live question is whether the Bible itself teaches one God in three co-equal persons, or whether that formula emerged later, in vocabulary the biblical authors never used. For a Muslim reader, it is how to describe Christian belief accurately while presenting the Quran's uncompromising monotheism. For a seeker between the two, it is the oldest question there is: who, exactly, is God?

The method follows the rest of this library: present each side from its own sources, quote scripture exactly as published translations render it, then compare.

sections.title: How the Doctrine Was Defined: Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381)
sections.body:
The word “Trinity” does not appear anywhere in the Bible. The New Testament speaks constantly of God the Father, of Jesus the Son, and of the Holy Spirit, but nowhere states a formula of “one God in three co-equal persons.” Christian scholarship itself acknowledges that the formal doctrine took shape gradually across the first four centuries.

The turning point was a fourth-century dispute. Arius, a church elder in Alexandria, taught that the Son was God's first and most exalted creature — supremely honored, but not eternal and not equal to the Father. The dispute spread so widely that the Roman emperor Constantine convened a council of bishops at Nicaea in the year 325 CE. According to the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's historical survey, the council met in mid-325 with perhaps 220 bishops; it condemned Arius's teaching and issued a creed declaring the Son homoousios — one in essence — with the Father. Arius and a few supporters refused to sign and were exiled.

Nicaea did not end the argument. Debate over the creed's wording and the status of the Holy Spirit continued for decades. Around the Council of Constantinople in 381 CE, the trinitarian belief that ultimately prevailed, in the Stanford Encyclopedia's words, “began to gel into a recognizable form,” now backed by imperial authority; views that subordinated the Son were progressively suppressed. The expanded creed associated with this era — recited today as the Nicene Creed — confesses Father, Son, and Holy Spirit as together the one God.

Two readings of this history will matter later. Christian theologians describe the councils as clarifying what was implicit in scripture all along. Muslims read the same history as evidence that a philosophical doctrine, in Greek terms the prophets never used, gradually replaced the simple monotheism Jesus himself taught. The dates themselves — 325 and 381, roughly three centuries after Jesus — are not in dispute.

sections.title: The Quranic Perspective
sections.body:
The Quran addresses Christians directly, respectfully — as “People of the Scripture” — and with a clear correction. The fullest statement is Quran 4:171:

يَٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌۭ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًۭا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۖ سُبْحَٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًۭا

“O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, "Three"; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs.” (Quran 4:171, Saheeh International)

Notice what the verse affirms before what it denies. Jesus is called the Messiah, a messenger of God, God's word directed to Mary, and a soul created at His command — titles of immense honor. Then comes the boundary: “do not say, ‘Three.’” The Quran's objection is not to Jesus but to elevating him, or anyone, into the Godhead.

A second verse addresses the three-fold confession directly. Quran 5:73:

لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَٰثَةٍۢ ۘ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَٰهٍ إِلَّآ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۚ وَإِن لَّمْ يَنتَهُوا۟ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ لَيَمَسَّنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

“They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the third of three." And there is no god except one God. And if they do not desist from what they are saying, there will surely afflict the disbelievers among them a painful punishment.” (Quran 5:73, Saheeh International)

The Quran does not engage the church's technical vocabulary of essence and persons; it addresses the confession as ordinary believers voice it — God spoken of as one of three — and rules out every formula in which the one God's divinity is shared: “there is no god except one God.”

A third passage stages a scene from the Day of Judgment. Quran 5:116:

وَإِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ءَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ ٱتَّخِذُونِى وَأُمِّىَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ سُبْحَٰنَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِحَقٍّ ۚ إِن كُنتُ قُلْتُهُۥ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُۥ ۚ تَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِى وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِكَ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ

“And [beware the Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'" He will say, "Exalted are You! It was not for me to say that to which I have no right. If I had said it, You would have known it. You know what is within myself, and I do not know what is within Yourself. Indeed, it is You who is Knower of the unseen.” (Quran 5:116, Saheeh International)

Here Jesus himself disowns every claim to be worshiped: he does not even claim to know what is in God's self. The verse asks a question about worship as practiced — including veneration of his mother Mary — rather than quoting a church creed; the misconceptions section returns to this point.

Finally, Surah Al-Ikhlas (chapter 112), the Quran's most concentrated statement of who God is, answers the trinitarian formula line by line:

بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ

“Say, "He is Allah, [who is] One,” (Quran 112:1, Saheeh International)

ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ

“Allah, the Eternal Refuge.” (Quran 112:2, Saheeh International)

لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ

“He neither begets nor is born,” (Quran 112:3, Saheeh International)

وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌۢ

“Nor is there to Him any equivalent."” (Quran 112:4, Saheeh International)

“He neither begets nor is born” meets the creed's language of an eternally begotten Son head-on, and “nor is there to Him any equivalent” excludes any co-equal person. In four short verses the Quran states tawhid: God's absolute, indivisible oneness.

sections.title: The Biblical Texts: For and Against
sections.body:
Fairness requires hearing the passages Christians actually cite, in their own scripture. The most famous triadic text is the baptismal command at the end of Matthew's Gospel: “Go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,” (Matthew 28:19, World English Bible). Christians point out that one “name” — singular — is shared by Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

Paul closes a letter with a similar three-fold blessing: “The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, God’s love, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit, be with you all. Amen.” (2 Corinthians 13:14, World English Bible). Texts like these show that placing the three side by side is genuinely early, not a later invention.

The Gospel of John supplies the strongest material. Its opening line runs: “In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.” (John 1:1, World English Bible), and a few verses later: “The Word became flesh, and lived among us. We saw his glory, such glory as of the one and only Son of the Father, full of grace and truth.” (John 1:14, World English Bible) — the foundation of the doctrine of the incarnation, that the eternal Word became the man Jesus. And in a dispute with his opponents Jesus says: “I and the Father are one.” (John 10:30, World English Bible).

Yet the same scriptures contain passages that complicate the doctrine. In the same Gospel of John, Jesus tells his disciples: “You heard how I told you, ‘I go away, and I come to you.’ If you loved me, you would have rejoiced, because I said ‘I am going to my Father;’ for the Father is greater than I.” (John 14:28, World English Bible). And in Mark, speaking of the final hour, he says: “But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.” (Mark 13:32, World English Bible). A Son who calls the Father greater, and who does not know what the Father knows, sits uneasily beside co-equality.

Mainstream Christian theology answers by distinguishing Jesus's two natures: as God he is equal and all-knowing; as man, lesser and limited. Interpreters likewise debate whether the oneness of John 10:30 is a oneness of essence or of will and purpose. These are serious answers, offered in good faith. Whether they are the most natural reading of the texts is the question the next section takes up.

sections.title: Weighing the Two Positions
sections.body:
Begin with common ground. Christianity and Islam both confess one God — the disagreement is not monotheism versus polytheism, and it is unfair to call the Trinity belief in three gods. The question is whether the one God's inner life contains three co-equal persons, or whether God is one in an absolute, undifferentiated sense — tawhid.

Now test each claim against the texts quoted above. The triadic passages — Matthew 28:19 and 2 Corinthians 13:14 — genuinely name the three together, but naming three together is not the doctrine; neither verse states that the three are one being, co-equal and co-eternal. Those further claims required the councils' vocabulary — ousia, hypostasis, homoousios — which appears nowhere in the Bible. John's Gospel brings the reader closest: “the Word was God.” Yet the same Gospel has Jesus say “the Father is greater than I,” and Mark has the Son not knowing the day or the hour. The two-natures explanation can absorb these verses, but only by adding another layer of post-biblical doctrine.

The Quranic position requires no such machinery. God is one; Jesus is His honored messenger, word, and servant. On that reading, “the Father is greater than I” and “only the Father” knows the hour mean exactly what they say. Every verse quoted in this article — from either scripture — is consistent with the sentence “Indeed, Allah is but one God” (Quran 4:171). The trinitarian must explain the subordination texts; the affirmer of God's oneness need only explain honorific language about a uniquely honored prophet — language the Quran itself uses of Jesus.

That asymmetry — which side needs the more elaborate auxiliary doctrine — is, in the Islamic argument, decisive.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
Misconception one: “Christians worship three gods.” They do not, and Muslims who say so misdescribe their neighbors. Every mainstream church condemns tritheism as heresy. The Islamic critique is that the formula “one God in three persons” compromises God's oneness in a way no prophet taught, however sincerely its holders affirm monotheism.

Misconception two: “The Quran misunderstands the Trinity as Father, Jesus, and Mary” — an objection usually aimed at Quran 5:116. Read the verse again: it is a question posed to Jesus on the Day of Judgment about worship offered to him and his mother, not a definition of the church's creed. The Quran's concern is worship as practiced — devotion directed to anyone besides God — and devotion to Mary was, and in some traditions remains, a live feature of Christian piety. Where the Quran addresses the three-fold confession, in 4:171 and 5:73, it says “do not say, ‘Three’” and “third of three,” without naming Mary at all.

Misconception three: “The Trinity is stated plainly in the Bible.” The word never appears, and the developed formula was defined at Nicaea and Constantinople, as the historical section showed. One verse that once seemed to state the doctrine outright — the “three heavenly witnesses” wording found at 1 John 5:7 in some older translations — is regarded by modern textual scholars across confessional lines as a later addition, and modern Bibles omit it or flag it in a footnote.

Misconception four: “Muslims reject the Trinity because they dishonor Jesus.” The opposite is true: no Muslim's faith is valid without belief in Jesus as the Messiah, God's messenger, born of a virgin. The Quran denies him nothing except divinity — and reports him denying that himself.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
The Islamic conclusion can be stated gently. The Quran does not ask Christians to abandon Jesus; it asks them to return to him — to the prayerful, dependent, obedient servant of God the Gospels themselves portray, who calls the Father greater, confesses that only God knows the hour, and directs all worship to the One who sent him.

Islam's claim is that the simple creed — “Say, ‘He is Allah, [who is] One’” (Quran 112:1) — is the original message of every prophet, Jesus included. On the Islamic reading, the Trinity is a sincere but mistaken development, assembled from real scriptural honor for Jesus and Greek philosophical categories, formalized by councils in 325 and 381 — not in the preaching of Jesus.

The invitation of Quran 4:171 is therefore precise: “do not commit excess in your religion.” Honor the Messiah as much as truth allows — messenger, word from God, spirit from Him — and stop exactly at the line he himself never crossed: “Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son.” For the seeker weighing both faiths, the question to carry away is this: which description of God do the words of Jesus, taken at face value, actually fit?

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Christians believe in three gods?
A: No. Mainstream Christianity condemns tritheism as heresy. The doctrine of the Trinity claims one God in three co-equal persons — and the Islamic critique addresses that actual claim, not a caricature.

Q: Does the word “Trinity” appear in the Bible?
A: No. The New Testament names the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit together in verses like Matthew 28:19, but the word “Trinity” and the formula “one essence, three persons” come from later centuries of theology.

Q: What do the Councils of Nicaea and Constantinople have to do with it?
A: Nicaea (325 CE) declared the Son homoousios — one in essence — with the Father, against Arius, who taught the Son was a created being. Around Constantinople (381 CE) the full trinitarian position, including the Holy Spirit, became mainstream Christianity's established doctrine.

Q: Does the Quran say the Trinity is Father, Jesus, and Mary?
A: No. Quran 5:116 records a Judgment-Day question to Jesus about people worshiping him and his mother; it is about misdirected worship, not a definition of the creed. Where the Quran addresses the three-fold confession (4:171, 5:73) it simply says: do not say “Three.”

Q: If Muslims honor Jesus, why reject the Trinity?
