A: Because the highest honor for Jesus is the truth about him: Messiah, messenger, word and spirit from God — but a worshiper of God, not God. Surah Al-Ikhlas draws the line: God neither begets nor is born, and nothing is equivalent to Him.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below are starting points from both traditions, listed as further reading rather than as sources quoted in this article.

From Christian and academic scholarship: Dale Tuggy's “Trinity” entry in the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (the reference used in this article for the doctrine's definition and the council dates); J. N. D. Kelly's Early Christian Doctrines on how the doctrine developed; and Thomas F. Torrance's The Christian Doctrine of God, One Being Three Persons as a modern statement of the doctrine from within the faith.

On the historical development of beliefs about Jesus: Bart D. Ehrman's How Jesus Became God, written by a historian for general readers.

From Islamic scholarship: Muhammad Abduh's The Theology of Unity, a classic modern presentation of tawhid.

relatedArticles.title: What Is Tawhid?
relatedArticles.subtitle: The Oneness of God: Islam's Foundational Doctrine and the Message of Every Prophet
relatedArticles.summary: Tawhid is the Islamic doctrine of the oneness of God: He alone is Lord, He alone deserves worship, and nothing is like Him. Muslims believe it was the message of every prophet, from Noah and Abraham to Moses and Jesus. This article defines Tawhid and its opposite, shirk, in plain language for beginners. It then quotes the Quran and the Bible side by side, including the Shema of Moses and the words of Jesus in the Gospel of Mark.

sections.title: In Plain Words
sections.body:
Tawhid (pronounced taw-HEED) is the Arabic word for the oneness of God, and it is the single most important teaching in Islam. It means that there is only one God, that He alone has the right to be worshipped, that He alone is the Lord and Sustainer of everything, and that nothing in creation is like Him.

Muslims believe Tawhid was not invented in the seventh century. They believe it was the one message carried by every prophet God ever sent, from Noah and Abraham to Moses, Jesus, and finally Muhammad, peace be upon them all.

This article explains what Tawhid means in plain language, introduces its opposite, called shirk, and then lets the texts speak for themselves: first the Quran, and then the Bible, where the same theme of God's exclusive oneness appears in the words of Moses, Isaiah, and Jesus himself.

sections.title: Introduction: The Center of Islam
sections.body:
Every religion has a center of gravity. In Islam, that center is not a ritual, a place, or even a prophet. It is a statement about God: la ilaha illa Allah, "there is no deity worthy of worship except God." The doctrine behind that statement is called Tawhid, from an Arabic root meaning "to make one" or "to affirm as one."

Muslim scholars have traditionally unpacked Tawhid into three connected aspects, and learning them makes everything else in Islam easier to understand. The first is the oneness of God's lordship (in Arabic, tawhid al-rububiyyah): God alone creates, sustains, gives life, and governs the universe, with no helper, rival, or committee. The second is the oneness of worship (tawhid al-uluhiyyah): because God alone is Lord, He alone deserves prayer, devotion, sacrifice, and ultimate love. Worship, in other words, is God's exclusive right. The third is the oneness of His names and attributes (tawhid al-asma wa-l-sifat): God is described by the most beautiful names, such as the Most Merciful and the All-Knowing, and His perfection is His alone; nothing in creation resembles Him.

These three aspects are teaching categories, not three separate doctrines. In the Quran they are woven together into a single, seamless picture of one God who made you, who sustains you, and who asks for your heart undivided.

sections.title: The Question: What Does "One God" Really Mean?
sections.body:
The question this article answers is simple to ask and profound to think through: what exactly does Islam mean when it says God is one, and was that oneness the original message of all the prophets, including Moses and Jesus?

"One" here is not merely a number, as if the only issue were counting. Tawhid means God is one in an exclusive and absolute sense: no partners who share His power, no intermediaries who receive a portion of worship, no offspring who inherit His nature, and no image or likeness that captures Him. It is oneness of being, oneness of authority, and oneness of the right to be worshipped, all at once.

Tawhid also has an opposite, and the Quran treats it with the utmost seriousness. It is called shirk, which means associating partners with God: directing any act of worship to something other than Him, believing that anything shares His lordship, or giving His unique attributes to a created being. Shirk can be as obvious as bowing to a statue or as subtle as making wealth, power, or one's own desires the true object of devotion. In Islam it is the gravest of all sins, because it gives away the one thing that belongs to God alone.

sections.title: One Message, Many Prophets
sections.body:
The Quran does not present Islam as a new religion founded in seventh-century Arabia. It presents Islam as the restoration of the original faith of humanity: surrender to the one God. In the Quran's telling, history is a long chain of prophets, each sent to his own people, each carrying the same core message of Tawhid, and each followed, in time, by communities that drifted from it.

The Hebrew Bible preserves that same core message in some of its most famous lines. The declaration known as the Shema, from the Book of Deuteronomy, is the closest thing Judaism has to a creed; observant Jews have recited it in daily prayer for many centuries, and it declares in a single breath that God is one. The Ten Commandments open not with a rule about honesty or family, but with God's exclusive claim to worship. The prophet Isaiah repeats the theme with striking force: there is no god besides the one God.

The Gospels then record something remarkable for this discussion. When Jesus was asked which commandment is the greatest of all, he did not announce a new doctrine of God. He recited the Shema of Moses. Centuries after Jesus, church councils formulated the doctrine of the Trinity, the teaching that the one God exists as three persons; Christians regard this as the correct interpretation of their scriptures. Islam, arriving after those debates, called people back to what it presents as the older, simpler creed: the unqualified oneness proclaimed by every prophet. The sections below quote the texts on which that claim rests.

sections.title: Tawhid in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran's most concentrated statement of Tawhid is its 112th chapter, Surah al-Ikhlas, "Purity of Faith." It is only four verses long, and Muslim tradition holds that it summarizes the Quran's entire teaching about who God is. Its first verse reads (the Arabic below opens with the basmala, "In the name of God, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful," the phrase with which the chapter begins):

بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ

"Say, "He is Allah, [who is] One," (Quran 112:1)

ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ

"Allah, the Eternal Refuge." (Quran 112:2)

The word translated "Eternal Refuge" is al-Samad: the One on whom all creation depends, while He depends on nothing and no one. Then come two verses that draw the boundary lines of Tawhid with complete precision:

لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ

"He neither begets nor is born," (Quran 112:3)

وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌۢ

"Nor is there to Him any equivalent."" (Quran 112:4)

God has no parents and no offspring, no origin and no heirs, and nothing anywhere is comparable to Him. In four short verses, the chapter rules out idolatry, polytheism, and any doctrine that gives God a son or an equal.

Is this oneness unique to the Quran's own message? The Quran says no. It insists that Tawhid was the message of every messenger God ever sent:

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدُونِ

"And We sent not before you any messenger except that We revealed to him that, "There is no deity except Me, so worship Me."" (Quran 21:25)

وَلَقَدْ بَعَثْنَا فِى كُلِّ أُمَّةٍۢ رَّسُولًا أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلطَّٰغُوتَ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ ٱلضَّلَٰلَةُ ۚ فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ

"And We certainly sent into every nation a messenger, [saying], "Worship Allah and avoid Taghut." And among them were those whom Allah guided, and among them were those upon whom error was [deservedly] decreed. So proceed through the earth and observe how was the end of the deniers." (Quran 16:36)

The word Taghut in this verse means false objects of worship: anything served or deified in place of God. According to these verses, no nation on earth was left without a messenger, and every messenger preached the same two-part call that defines Tawhid: worship God, and renounce whatever is worshipped besides Him.

The Quran is equally careful about how we may describe God. He is utterly unlike His creation, yet He is not an abstraction; He hears and sees:

فَاطِرُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًۭا وَمِنَ ٱلْأَنْعَٰمِ أَزْوَٰجًۭا ۖ يَذْرَؤُكُمْ فِيهِ ۚ لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِۦ شَىْءٌۭ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ

"[He is] Creator of the heavens and the earth. He has made for you from yourselves, mates, and among the cattle, mates; He multiplies you thereby. There is nothing like unto Him, and He is the Hearing, the Seeing." (Quran 42:11)

That single verse contains the balance Muslim theology strives to keep: nothing is like God, and yet He is genuinely knowable through the names and attributes He reveals about Himself. The Quran calls these the most beautiful names:

ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ

"Allah - there is no deity except Him. To Him belong the best names." (Quran 20:8)

One passage near the end of Surah al-Hashr gathers many of these names into a crescendo that Muslims often recite:

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَٰدَةِ ۖ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, Knower of the unseen and the witnessed. He is the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful." (Quran 59:22)

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

"He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer, the Exalted in Might, the Compeller, the Superior. Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him." (Quran 59:23)

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

"He is Allah, the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner; to Him belong the best names. Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise." (Quran 59:24)

Notice what these verses accomplish together. They keep God transcendent, exalted above whatever is associated with Him, and at the same time intimately describable: merciful, sovereign, pure, creating, fashioning, wise. Tawhid is not the worship of a distant force; it is a personal relationship with the one Lord who has told us who He is.

Finally, the Quran states the seriousness of Tawhid's opposite in the plainest possible terms:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدِ ٱفْتَرَىٰٓ إِثْمًا عَظِيمًا

"Indeed, Allah does not forgive association with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills. And he who associates others with Allah has certainly fabricated a tremendous sin." (Quran 4:48)

Shirk, the association of partners with God, is the one sin the Quran singles out this way, because it violates the very purpose for which, in Islam, human beings exist. The door of repentance remains open in this life to anyone who turns back to God; the warning concerns those who die still insisting on it.

sections.title: The Oneness of God in the Bible
sections.body:
A reader who opens the Bible looking for the theme of God's oneness does not have to search long. The Torah, the Prophets, and the Gospels each contain declarations that Muslims immediately recognize as the language of Tawhid. All quotations below are from the World English Bible translation.

The most famous is the Shema, the verse at the heart of Jewish faith and prayer: "Hear, Israel: Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one." (Deuteronomy 6:4). One God, stated without qualification or elaboration. This is the creed Moses delivered to Israel.

The Ten Commandments begin the same way, with God's exclusive claim on worship: "“I am Yahweh your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage. “You shall have no other gods before me." (Exodus 20:2-3). Before any command about human conduct comes the command that defines all the others: no other gods. Worship belongs to God alone, which is precisely the second aspect of Tawhid described earlier.

The prophet Isaiah presses the point to its logical end, ruling out not just the worship of other gods but their very existence: "I am Yahweh, and there is no one else. Besides me, there is no God. I will strengthen you, though you have not known me;" (Isaiah 45:5).

Then comes the passage that matters most for a conversation between Muslims and Christians. In the Gospel of Mark, a scribe asks Jesus which commandment is the greatest of all. Mark records: "Jesus answered, “The greatest is, ‘Hear, Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one:" (Mark 12:29). The verse continues into the command to love God with all one's heart, soul, mind, and strength. Asked to name the very core of faith, Jesus answered with the Shema of Moses: the Lord our God, the Lord is one.

Christians, of course, hold all of these verses sacred and read them in harmony with the doctrine of the Trinity, which they understand as monotheism: one God in three persons, not three gods. That reading deserves to be stated fairly, and it is examined on its own terms elsewhere in this library. The observation made here is more modest: the verses themselves, as they stand, proclaim God's oneness in simple, exclusive, unqualified terms, and that is exactly what Islam means by Tawhid.

sections.title: Side by Side: Tawhid and the Trinity
sections.body:
It helps to state each position honestly and side by side.

Islam holds that God is one in an absolute and undifferentiated sense: one divine being, with no distinction of persons within Him, no incarnation in human flesh, and no partner, offspring, or equal. Worship of anyone or anything besides Him, including any prophet or saint, is shirk. On this view, Jesus was a mighty messenger of God who taught the same creed as Moses, and devotion belongs to the God Jesus worshipped.

Mainstream Christianity holds that God is one being in three co-equal, co-eternal persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. Christians insist, and have always insisted, that this is monotheism, not the worship of three gods; the Trinity is presented as a deeper description of the one God's inner life, with the Son becoming incarnate in Jesus.

The texts quoted in this article say: "Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one"; "You shall have no other gods before me"; "I am Yahweh, and there is no one else"; and, from the lips of Jesus, "the Lord our God, the Lord is one." On the Quran's side: "He is Allah, [who is] One"; "He neither begets nor is born, nor is there to Him any equivalent"; "There is no deity except Me, so worship Me."

The Islamic argument is straightforward. Read on their own, these texts express oneness that needs no further doctrinal machinery: no persons to distinguish, no natures to unite, no councils required to settle the definition. A Muslim can affirm every word of the Shema, the first commandment, Isaiah's declaration, and Jesus's answer to the scribe, exactly as written. Tawhid, Muslims argue, is not a rival to the message of the Bible's prophets; it is that message, preserved in its original simplicity.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few misunderstandings come up so often that they deserve direct answers.

First: "Allah is a different god from the God of the Bible." Allah is simply the Arabic word for God, related to the Hebrew Elohim and the Aramaic Alaha. Arabic-speaking Christians and Jews use the word Allah for God in their own prayers and Bibles. When Muslims say Allah, they mean the God of Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus: the Creator of the heavens and the earth.

Second: "A God so transcendent must be cold and distant." The same Quran that says nothing is like God also names Him the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful, the Hearing, the Seeing. Tawhid removes intermediaries precisely so that every person can turn to God directly, with no priesthood or broker in between.

Third: "Muslims accuse Christians of worshipping three gods." That is not the careful Islamic position, and this library does not argue that way. Christian doctrine explicitly affirms one God, and honesty requires saying so. The Islamic critique is different: that the Trinity, however sincerely intended as monotheism, goes beyond the simple oneness that the quoted texts proclaim, and that the safer path is to stay with what Moses and Jesus actually said.

Fourth: "Shirk just means bowing to idols." Idolatry is its clearest form, but shirk includes any act of worship directed to other than God: praying to saints, seeking from the dead what only God can give, or making money, status, or desire one's functional god.

Fifth: "Muslims worship the Kaaba in Mecca." Muslims pray toward the Kaaba as a single direction that unites worshippers worldwide, the way the Bible describes prophets praying toward Jerusalem. No Muslim worships the building; worship is for God alone.

sections.title: Conclusion: One God, One Message
sections.body:
Tawhid is Islam's answer to the oldest and most important question a human being can ask: who deserves my worship? The answer is the One who made you, sustains you every second, and will one day call you back: God alone, without partner, without son, without equal.

What gives that answer its force is its consistency. The Quran proclaims it: there is no deity except Him. The Torah proclaims it: the Lord is one; you shall have no other gods. The prophets proclaim it: besides Me there is no God. And when Jesus himself was asked to name the greatest commandment, he reached for the same creed. Islam asks the reader to take that pattern seriously. Either the prophets shared one message, or they did not; the texts, quoted throughout this article, speak with one voice.

For the Muslim, Tawhid is not an abstract doctrine but a way of standing in the world: one God to please rather than a thousand masters to appease, one door to knock on, one judge whose verdict matters. It is, Muslims believe, the faith of Abraham in its purest form, open to anyone willing to say with conviction that there is no deity worthy of worship except God. The invitation of this entire library begins here, because in Islam everything else follows from this.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: What does the word Tawhid literally mean?
A: Tawhid comes from an Arabic root meaning "to make one" or "to affirm as one." As a doctrine, it means affirming that God is one in His lordship, one in His exclusive right to worship, and unique in His names and attributes.

Q: Is Allah the same God that Jews and Christians worship?
A: Allah is simply the Arabic word for God, and Arabic-speaking Jews and Christians use it themselves. Muslims understand Allah to be the God of Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. The three faiths describe God differently in doctrine, but the reference is to the Creator worshipped by the biblical prophets.

Q: What is shirk, and why is it so serious in Islam?
A: Shirk means associating partners with God: giving worship, ultimate devotion, or God's unique attributes to anything created. The Quran calls it the one sin God does not forgive for a person who dies upon it without repenting, because it gives away the sole right for which humans were created. Sincere repentance in this life wipes it away.

Q: Did Jesus teach the oneness of God?
A: According to the Gospel of Mark, when Jesus was asked which commandment is the greatest, he recited the Shema: "Hear, Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one." Muslims see in this the same creed of Tawhid taught by Moses and every prophet, while Christians read it in harmony with later Trinitarian doctrine.

Q: Do Muslims believe Christians worship three gods?
A: No. Christian doctrine explicitly teaches one God in three persons, and fairness requires representing it that way. The Islamic critique is not that Christians intend polytheism, but that the Trinity adds distinctions to God's oneness that the plain declarations of the Torah, the Prophets, and Jesus do not contain.

Q: What are the three categories of Tawhid?
A: Scholars traditionally teach Tawhid in three aspects: oneness of lordship (God alone creates and sustains), oneness of worship (God alone deserves devotion), and oneness of names and attributes (God's perfection is unique and incomparable). They are three angles on a single truth, not three separate doctrines.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following well-known works are offered as further reading for readers who want to go deeper. They are listed for orientation, not as sources for specific claims made in this article.

Abu Ameenah Bilal Philips — The Fundamentals of Tawheed: a widely read English-language introduction to the categories of Tawhid and the forms of shirk.

Imam Abu Ja'far al-Tahawi — The Creed of Imam al-Tahawi (al-Aqidah al-Tahawiyyah): a classical summary of Muslim beliefs about God, accepted across the major Sunni schools.

Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab — Kitab al-Tawhid: a classical thematic collection on the oneness of worship and warnings against shirk.

Hamza Andreas Tzortzis — The Divine Reality: a contemporary case for God's existence and oneness addressed to modern readers, including atheists and agnostics.

relatedArticles.title: Who Is God? The Quran and the Bible Compared
relatedArticles.subtitle: What the two scriptures actually say about God's names, oneness, mercy, justice, and nearness to humanity
relatedArticles.summary: The Quran and the Bible both describe one eternal Creator who is merciful, just, all-knowing, and utterly unlike His creation. This article places the two scriptures side by side, quoting each in full, to show where they agree deeply and where they part ways. The agreements — one God, everlasting, merciful and just — are far larger than many people expect. The differences center on whether God ever became human, whether He can be seen, and what 'fatherhood' and 'begetting' language means.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Both the Quran and the Bible teach that there is one God: eternal, the Creator of everything, all-knowing, merciful, and just. On these foundations the two books sound remarkably alike, and this article quotes the actual texts so you can see it.

They differ on three main points. Mainstream Christianity teaches that God exists as a Trinity — one God in three persons — and that the Son became human in Jesus; Islam teaches that God is absolutely one and never becomes part of His creation. The Quran says no vision can grasp God in this life, while Christian theology holds that God made Himself visible in Christ. And the Bible uses 'Father' and 'Son' language that the Quran deliberately avoids, insisting that God 'neither begets nor is born.'

Every verse below is quoted word for word from the Saheeh International translation of the Quran and the World English Bible.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
Ask a Muslim and a Christian to describe God and, for the first several sentences, you may not be able to tell them apart. One God. Creator of the heavens and the earth. Eternal, without beginning or end. Merciful beyond measure, yet perfectly just. Nothing in creation resembles Him.

Then come the sentences where the two descriptions divide — and those differences matter enormously, because they concern the very identity of the One both communities worship.

This article lets each scripture speak for itself: every Quranic verse below is quoted in Arabic with the Saheeh International translation, and every biblical passage from the World English Bible, a public-domain translation of the Hebrew and Greek. We will look at God's names, His oneness, His mercy and justice, His transcendence (a word that simply means God is above and beyond His creation, not limited by it), and His relationship with human beings — and only after both scriptures have been heard fairly will we compare them.

sections.title: The Question We Are Really Asking
sections.body:
The question 'who is God?' breaks into smaller, concrete questions that both scriptures answer directly. Is God one — and in what sense? What is God like: what are His names and attributes (an 'attribute' is simply a quality God has, such as mercy or knowledge)? Is He primarily merciful, primarily just, or both at once? Can He be seen, imagined, or represented? Does He ever take on a created form? And how does He relate to us — as a distant force, a loving parent, a master, a judge?

A fair comparison has to notice something honest at the outset: Muslims and Christians are not arguing about whether there is one eternal Creator. Both firmly say yes. The real disagreement is about what that one God is like and whether He ever entered His own creation. Keeping that distinction clear prevents the most common caricatures on both sides — that Christians worship three gods (they say they do not), or that Muslims worship some other, foreign deity ('Allah' is simply the Arabic word for God).

sections.title: Where These Texts Come From
sections.body:
The Bible is not a single book but a library written over many centuries. The Hebrew Bible (which Christians call the Old Testament) was composed mainly in Hebrew and contains the passages about God's oneness, eternity, and mercy quoted below; Deuteronomy 6:4, known in Judaism as the Shema, remains the central Jewish declaration of faith recited daily. The New Testament was written in Greek in the first century CE by followers of Jesus.

Christian doctrine about God's inner nature developed over the following centuries. The classic formulation of the Trinity — one God in three co-equal persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — was hammered out in church councils, beginning with the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE and continuing through later fourth-century councils. Christians regard this doctrine as the correct interpretation of their scriptures, not an addition to them; that is how their own scholarship describes it, and a fair reader should know that.

The Quran was proclaimed by the Prophet Muhammad in seventh-century Arabia over roughly two decades. It presents itself not as a new religion's founding document but as a restoration of the original message of all prophets — Abraham, Moses, and Jesus included — centered on the absolute oneness of God, called Tawhid in Arabic. Strikingly, the Quran speaks directly to Jews and Christians ('People of the Book'), affirming that their God and its God are the same, while correcting what it presents as later misunderstandings about His nature.

sections.title: God in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran's most concentrated answer to our question is a four-verse chapter, Surah Al-Ikhlaas (chapter 112), which Muslims regard as a summary of the entire doctrine of God. In the printed Quran its first verse is preceded by the basmala — the opening formula 'In the name of God' — which appears at the start of the Arabic below.

بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ

"Say, 'He is Allah, [who is] One," (Quran 112:1)

ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ

"Allah, the Eternal Refuge." (Quran 112:2)

لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ

"He neither begets nor is born," (Quran 112:3)

وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌۢ

"Nor is there to Him any equivalent." (Quran 112:4)

Four claims in forty seconds of recitation: God is one (ahad — a oneness that admits no internal division); He is al-Samad, the self-sufficient One upon whom everything depends while He depends on nothing; He has no parent and no offspring; and nothing whatsoever resembles Him.

The Quran also gives God many names — traditionally counted as ninety-nine — each naming an attribute. A famous passage in Surah Al-Hashr strings a series of them together.

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَٰدَةِ ۖ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, Knower of the unseen and the witnessed. He is the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful." (Quran 59:22)

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

"He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer, the Exalted in Might, the Compeller, the Superior. Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him." (Quran 59:23)

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

"He is Allah, the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner; to Him belong the best names. Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise." (Quran 59:24)

Notice the order: before power and majesty, mercy. 'The Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful' (al-Rahman, al-Rahim) is the description Muslims repeat before almost every action of their day. The Quran invites believers to call on God through all these names.

وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ فَٱدْعُوهُ بِهَا ۖ وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ أَسْمَٰٓئِهِۦ ۚ سَيُجْزَوْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

"And to Allah belong the best names, so invoke Him by them. And leave [the company of] those who practice deviation concerning His names. They will be recompensed for what they have been doing." (Quran 7:180)

At the same time, the Quran guards God's transcendence fiercely. He is nearer to us than our own selves in knowledge and care, yet He cannot be captured by human sight or imagination in this life.

لَّا تُدْرِكُهُ ٱلْأَبْصَٰرُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ ٱلْأَبْصَٰرَ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ

"Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives [all] vision; and He is the Subtle, the Acquainted." (Quran 6:103)

And while everything in creation is passing, God alone remains.

كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍۢ

"Everyone upon the earth will perish," (Quran 55:26)

وَيَبْقَىٰ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو ٱلْجَلَٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ

"And there will remain the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor." (Quran 55:27)

That is the Quranic portrait: one God, uncreated and unending, supremely merciful and perfectly just, intimately aware of every creature, and absolutely beyond comparison with anything He has made.

sections.title: God in the Bible
sections.body:
The Bible's portrait of God begins with His self-identification to Moses at the burning bush: 'God said to Moses, "I AM WHO I AM," and he said, "You shall tell the children of Israel this: 'I AM has sent me to you.'"' (Exodus 3:14). God is the One who simply is — self-existent, depending on nothing. Readers may notice how close this stands to the Quran's al-Samad, the Eternal Refuge on whom all things depend.

The foundational creed of the Hebrew Bible is the Shema: 'Hear, Israel: Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one.' (Deuteronomy 6:4). Yahweh is the personal name of God in the Hebrew text; the World English Bible renders it directly. This verse remains the heartbeat of Jewish faith, and Christians affirm it too.

God's eternity is sung in the Psalms: 'Before the mountains were born, before you had formed the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, you are God.' (Psalms 90:2). The prophet Isaiah adds His inexhaustible power and knowledge: 'Haven't you known? Haven't you heard? The everlasting God, Yahweh, The Creator of the ends of the earth, doesn't faint. He isn't weary. His understanding is unsearchable.' (Isaiah 40:28).

What is this eternal Creator like in character? The Hebrew Bible's fullest answer comes when God proclaims His own attributes to Moses: 'Yahweh passed by before him, and proclaimed, "Yahweh! Yahweh, a merciful and gracious God, slow to anger, and abundant in loving kindness and truth, keeping loving kindness for thousands, forgiving iniquity and disobedience and sin; and who will by no means clear the guilty, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children, and on the children's children, on the third and on the fourth generation."' (Exodus 34:6-7). Mercy comes first and overflows — yet justice is not cancelled. Anyone who knows the Quran's pairing of al-Rahman al-Rahim with al-'Aziz (the Exalted in Might) will recognize the same balance.

The Hebrew Bible also insists that God is not like His creatures: 'God is not a man, that he should lie, nor a son of man, that he should repent. Has he said, and will he not do it? Or has he spoken, and will he not make it good?' (Numbers 23:19). And in the New Testament, Jesus himself teaches that 'God is spirit, and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth.' (John 4:24) — God is not physical, not confined to a place or a form.

Alongside all this, the New Testament speaks of God as 'Father' and of Jesus as God's 'Son,' language that mainstream Christian theology developed into the doctrine of the Trinity. On the Christian account — and fairness requires stating it as Christians state it — this is still monotheism: one divine being, one God, existing eternally as three persons. 'Begotten,' in the classical creeds, is explicitly said to mean an eternal relationship within God, not that God physically fathered a child.

sections.title: Side by Side: Agreement and Difference
sections.body:
Start with the common ground, because it is enormous. Both scriptures declare God's oneness: 'Say, "He is Allah, [who is] One' (Quran 112:1) and 'Hear, Israel: Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one.' (Deuteronomy 6:4). Both declare His eternity: 'from everlasting to everlasting, you are God' (Psalms 90:2) alongside 'Everyone upon the earth will perish, And there will remain the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor' (Quran 55:26-27). Both name Him the all-knowing Creator: 'The Creator of the ends of the earth... His understanding is unsearchable' (Isaiah 40:28) and 'the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner... the Exalted in Might, the Wise' (Quran 59:24). Both put mercy at the front of His self-description — 'a merciful and gracious God, slow to anger' (Exodus 34:6) and 'the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful' (Quran 59:22) — while both also affirm that He 'will by no means clear the guilty' (Exodus 34:7) and 'will be recompensed' every deed (Quran 7:180).

Now the differences, stated plainly. Islam holds that God's oneness is absolute and indivisible: no persons within God, no incarnation (God taking on a human body and nature), no son in any sense. Mainstream Christianity holds that the one God exists eternally as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, and that the Son became human in Jesus of Nazareth — one God, three persons, as Christians themselves define their monotheism.

The texts say the following. The Quran states that God 'neither begets nor is born' and has no 'equivalent' (Quran 112:3-4), and that 'Vision perceives Him not' (Quran 6:103). The Hebrew Bible states that 'God is not a man, that he should lie, nor a son of man' (Numbers 23:19) and Jesus states that 'God is spirit' (John 4:24). Christians respond that the incarnation does not make God 'a man' by nature — rather, the eternal Son assumed a human nature in addition to his divine nature, and 'begotten' in the creeds means an eternal, non-physical relationship. That is a genuine and sophisticated position, and it deserves to be engaged rather than mocked.

The Islamic engagement is this: every verse quoted above — from both books — describes God without triunity, incarnation, or divine sonship. The Shema needs no footnote. Exodus 34's portrait of mercy and justice is complete without a cross. Numbers 23:19 draws the line between God and man exactly where the Quran draws it. The Trinity is a later interpretive framework laid over these texts; the Quran's description requires no such framework, and on the plain sense of the shared scriptures, the simpler description is the one both books state outright.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
'Allah is a different god from the God of the Bible.' The word 'Allah' is simply the Arabic word for God — the same word Arabic-speaking Christians use in their Bibles and church services today, and a close cousin of the Hebrew words for God (Eloah, Elohim). The Quran explicitly identifies the God it proclaims with the God of Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. Whether Muslims and Christians describe that God correctly is the real debate; which deity they intend to worship is not.

'Christians worship three gods.' Mainstream Christianity has always rejected this. The doctrine of the Trinity claims one God in three persons, and Christian theologians call belief in three gods (tritheism) a heresy. Islam's critique is not that Christians knowingly worship three gods; it is that the Trinity compromises pure monotheism in effect, even though Christians intend monotheism.

'The God of the Quran is distant and unloving, while the God of the Bible is intimate.' The verses quoted above resist this. The Quran's most repeated divine names are 'the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful' (Quran 59:22), and it commands believers to call on God directly by His beautiful names (Quran 7:180) — no priest, no intermediary. Conversely, 'The God of the Bible is all wrath' fails too: Exodus 34:6-7 leads with mercy, grace, and abundant loving kindness before it mentions justice. Both books present a God who is both tender and just.

'The Quran denies that God has any attributes humans can relate to.' Not so. The Quran denies that God resembles creatures ('Nor is there to Him any equivalent,' Quran 112:4), but it fills its pages with relational names: the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer (Quran 59:23). Transcendence and closeness are affirmed together: vision cannot grasp Him, yet He is 'the Subtle, the Acquainted' (Quran 6:103) — aware of every detail of every life.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Set the two portraits side by side one last time. One God, eternal, self-existent, Creator of everything, all-knowing, merciful before all else yet never unjust, unlike anything He made. Up to this point the Quran and the Bible speak with one voice — and Islam says that this shared voice is the original message, taught by every prophet from Abraham to Moses to Jesus.

Where the two traditions diverge, the Islamic case rests on the texts themselves. 'He neither begets nor is born, nor is there to Him any equivalent' (Quran 112:3-4) does not contradict the Hebrew Bible; it echoes its plainest statements — 'Yahweh is one' (Deuteronomy 6:4), 'God is not a man... nor a son of man' (Numbers 23:19), 'God is spirit' (John 4:24). The Quran asks the reader to take those statements at face value, without the interpretive scaffolding of councils and creeds that came generations after Jesus.

This is why Islam describes itself not as a rival to the faith of Moses and Jesus but as its continuation and purification. Tawhid — the absolute oneness of God — is presented as the belief every prophet preached and every scripture, read plainly, still contains. The invitation of the Quran is correspondingly simple: return to worshiping the One whom Abraham worshiped, with nothing and no one alongside Him, calling on Him directly by His most beautiful names. He is, in the words both traditions can read together, the Everlasting Creator who does not faint or grow weary — and, in the Quran's closing image, when everything else has passed away, 'there will remain the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor' (Quran 55:27).

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims and Christians worship the same God?
A: Both communities intend to worship the God of Abraham — one eternal Creator. The word 'Allah' is simply Arabic for God and is used by Arabic-speaking Christians too. The disagreement is over God's nature (Trinity and incarnation versus absolute oneness), not over which Creator is meant.

Q: Does the Bible ever call God 'one' the way the Quran does?
A: Yes. Deuteronomy 6:4 — 'Hear, Israel: Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one.' — is the central creed of the Hebrew Bible, and Jesus and his earliest followers affirmed it. The Quran's Surah Al-Ikhlaas makes the same declaration with added precision: no begetting, no being begotten, no equivalent.

Q: Is the God of the Quran merciful, or mainly a judge?
A: The Quran's most frequent divine names are 'the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful' (Quran 59:22), recited by Muslims before nearly every daily act. Justice is equally real — deeds are recompensed — but Islamic teaching, like Exodus 34:6-7, places mercy first.

Q: Why does Islam reject calling God 'Father'?
A: The Quran avoids parent-child language for God because, in its view, such language historically slid into literal sonship and division within God. It states that God 'neither begets nor is born' (Quran 112:3). God's tenderness is expressed instead through names like the Entirely Merciful, the Subtle, the Acquainted.

Q: Can anyone see God, according to these scriptures?
A: The Quran says 'Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives [all] vision' (Quran 6:103) — God cannot be seen in this life, though Muslims believe the people of Paradise will see Him in the hereafter. The Bible teaches that 'God is spirit' (John 4:24), while mainstream Christianity holds that God made Himself visible in Jesus — one of the key points where the two faiths differ.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below are offered as general further reading for readers who want to go deeper. They are listed as starting points, not as sources for specific claims in this article; every scriptural quotation above comes directly from the cited translations (Saheeh International via api.alquran.cloud, and the World English Bible via bible-api.com).

Al-Ghazali — The Ninety-Nine Beautiful Names of God (al-Maqsad al-Asna): a classical Muslim exploration of the divine names quoted in Quran 7:180 and 59:22-24.

Tim Winter (ed.) — The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology: accessible academic essays on how Muslim scholars understood God's oneness and attributes.

Alister E. McGrath — Christian Theology: An Introduction: a standard, widely used textbook presenting the Trinity and incarnation as mainstream Christian scholarship understands them.

Karen Armstrong — A History of God: a readable comparative history of how Jews, Christians, and Muslims have understood the one God.

Gai Eaton — Islam and the Destiny of Man: a reflective introduction to the Islamic vision of God and humanity, written for Western readers.

title: Did Jesus Worship God?
subtitle: What the Gospel accounts and the Quran both record about the prayer life of Jesus — and what it means
summary: Both the Bible and the Quran record Jesus praying, fasting, and prostrating to God. The Gospels show him praying before dawn, all night on a mountainside, and face-down on the ground in Gethsemane, while the Quran quotes him saying 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah.' Mainstream Christianity understands this prayer as the incarnate Son communing with the Father within the Trinity; Islam understands it as the devotion of God's honored prophet and servant. This article quotes the key texts from both scriptures, presents both readings fairly, and then makes the Islamic case from the texts themselves.

sections.title: In a Nutshell
sections.body:
The four Gospels repeatedly describe Jesus praying: alone before dawn, all night on a mountainside, and face-down on the ground in the garden of Gethsemane on the night of his arrest. He fasted, he prostrated, and he taught that God alone deserves worship. The Quran describes the same man in one sentence: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah.'

This article gathers the actual texts — five passages from the Quran and seven from the Bible, each quoted word for word — and lets them speak. It then explains, fairly, how Christians and Muslims each understand a praying Jesus, and why Islam sees in his prayer life the simplest answer to the question of who he really was: not God receiving worship, but God's beloved prophet offering it.

sections.title: A Question Both Faiths Can Answer Together
sections.body:
Ask a Muslim and a Christian who Jesus is, and you will get two very different answers. Ask them whether Jesus prayed, and you will get the same answer from both. The Gospel writers mention his prayer life so often, and so casually, that it reads less like a claim being argued and more like a habit everyone around him knew: he slipped away before sunrise to pray, he spent whole nights in prayer, and in his final hours he fell on his face and pleaded with God.

The Quran describes the same pattern. In it, Jesus — called Isa in Arabic — introduces himself with the words 'I am the servant of Allah' and says that God 'has enjoined upon me prayer' for as long as he lives.

That agreement makes this question unusually valuable for a sincere seeker. Many debates about Jesus turn on disputed verses, Greek grammar, or complicated manuscript history. This one does not. The evidence sits on the surface of both scriptures, in plain narrative sentences. Our task is simply to gather those sentences and then ask, with fairness to both faiths, what a praying, fasting, prostrating Jesus tells us about who he was.

sections.title: What Exactly Are We Asking?
sections.body:
First, a definition. In both the Bible and the Quran, worship means devotion directed to God: prayer, fasting, prostration (bowing with the face to the ground), thanksgiving, and obedience offered to the Creator. The Arabic word is ibadah, and it comes from the same root as abd, meaning 'servant.' A worshiper is, by definition, a servant of the One being worshiped.

So the question 'Did Jesus worship God?' really contains two questions. The first is factual: do the records show Jesus performing acts of worship, and if so, toward whom did he direct them? The second is interpretive: if Jesus did worship God, what does that tell us about his identity? Worship flows in one direction — from the dependent to the One depended upon, from creature to Creator. A person who prays is asking; a person who fasts is submitting; a person who prostrates places himself low before another.

As we will see, Muslims and Christians largely agree on the factual answer. Where they part ways is on the second question — and that difference goes to the heart of what separates the two faiths.

sections.title: A Praying Jew in First-Century Palestine
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century in Roman-ruled Palestine, and he lived as a Jew. The everyday piety of a devout Jew in that world included regular prayer, fasting, worship at the synagogue and the Jerusalem Temple, and the declaration that God is one. The Gospels — Christianity's own foundational documents — show Jesus fully inside this world: he attends synagogue, keeps the festivals, fasts, and constantly quotes the Hebrew scriptures, including their commands to worship God alone.

It is also historically important that the formal doctrines about Jesus' divine nature were articulated after his lifetime, as Christianity spread through the Greek and Roman world. The Council of Nicaea, which met in 325 CE, declared the Son to be of the same substance as the Father, and a later council in the mid-fifth century, at Chalcedon, defined Jesus as one person with two complete natures, fully divine and fully human. Christians regard these councils as clarifying what the scriptures already taught. Muslims regard them as adding a layer of philosophy on top of a simpler original message.

Either way, one point matters for this article: the reports of Jesus praying are older than the councils that explained why he prayed. The prayer came first; the theology about the prayer came later. That order is worth keeping in mind as we turn to the texts themselves.

sections.title: Jesus in the Quran: The Servant Who Prays
sections.body:
The Quran mentions Jesus in dozens of verses and consistently presents him through one lens: he is God's honored Messiah, messenger, and servant, and his message was that God alone deserves worship. His very first words in the Quran — spoken miraculously as an infant in the cradle to defend his mother Mary — are a declaration of servanthood:

قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا

'[Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet.' (Quran 19:30, Saheeh International)

The Arabic phrase abd Allah means 'servant of Allah.' In Islam this is not a lowly title but the highest one a human being can hold. The infant Jesus continues:

وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّۭا

'And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive' (Quran 19:31, Saheeh International)

Notice what Jesus says God commanded him to do: salah (formal prayer) and zakah (obligatory charity) — the same core duties every Muslim carries. In the Quran's portrait, Jesus is not the object of worship; he is a worshiper, under God's command, for as long as he lives.

His message to others matched his own practice. Speaking to the Children of Israel, he says:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۗ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

'Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path."' (Quran 3:51, Saheeh International)

The Quran repeats this sentence, in almost identical words, as the summary of his teaching:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

'Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is a straight path."' (Quran 43:64, Saheeh International)

Twice, the same claim: God is 'my Lord and your Lord.' Jesus places himself on the same side of the Creator-creature line as his listeners. Finally, the Quran gives Jesus a speech on the Day of Judgment, when God asks him whether he ever told people to take him and his mother as gods besides Allah. Jesus answers:

مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَمَرْتَنِى بِهِۦٓ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۚ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِى كُنتَ أَنتَ ٱلرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ شَهِيدٌ

'I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness.' (Quran 5:117, Saheeh International)

For the Quran, then, the answer to our question is not a side detail. Jesus worshiping God, and calling everyone else to do the same, is the whole point of who Jesus is.

citations.note: Arabic text and translation fetched programmatically per verse; verify spot-checks against a printed mushaf and the published Saheeh International edition before final publication.

citations.note: Verse text fetched programmatically via bible-api.com; public-domain translation suitable for quotation.

sections.title: Jesus in the Gospels: A Life Full of Prayer
sections.body:
Now the Bible, quoted here from the World English Bible translation. What is striking is how strongly the Gospel narrative agrees with the Quranic portrait on the factual question. Mark, widely regarded by scholars as the earliest Gospel, describes Jesus' habit this way:

Early in the morning, while it was still dark, he rose up and went out, and departed into a deserted place, and prayed there. (Mark 1:35)

Luke reports the same pattern as routine:

But he withdrew himself into the desert, and prayed. (Luke 5:16)

These were not public performances. The texts stress that he prayed alone, in deserted places, before anyone was awake. Before choosing his twelve closest disciples, Luke tells us:

In these days, he went out to the mountain to pray, and he continued all night in prayer to God. (Luke 6:12)

Note Luke's own wording: all night in prayer 'to God.' The most intense scene comes on the night of his arrest, in the garden of Gethsemane:

He went forward a little, fell on his face, and prayed, saying, “My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass away from me; nevertheless, not what I desire, but what you desire.” (Matthew 26:39)

Here is prostration — falling on the face — the very posture Muslims take in every salah. And here is surrender: my desire yields to Yours. The Arabic word for that surrender of the will to God is islam.

The Letter to the Hebrews, reflecting back on Jesus' earthly life, sums it up in one remarkable sentence:

He, in the days of his flesh, having offered up prayers and petitions with strong crying and tears to him who was able to save him from death, and having been heard for his godly fear, (Hebrews 5:7)

Jesus offers prayers and petitions; God is the one 'able to save him'; and he is heard because of his reverence toward God.

In John's Gospel — the Gospel with the loftiest language about Jesus — Jesus prays aloud before his disciples:

Jesus said these things, and lifting up his eyes to heaven, he said, “Father, the time has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may also glorify you; even as you gave him authority over all flesh, so he will give eternal life to all whom you have given him. This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ. (John 17:1-3)

In his own prayer, Jesus calls the Father 'the only true God' and describes himself as the one 'whom you sent.' Finally, when the devil tempts him, Jesus states the rule that governs all worship:

Then Jesus said to him, “Get behind me, Satan! For it is written, ‘You shall worship the Lord your God, and you shall serve him only.’” (Matthew 4:10)

He is quoting the Torah, the scripture of his people: worship belongs to God alone.

sections.title: Two Readings of One Record
sections.body:
Islam holds that Jesus worshiped God because he was exactly what he said he was: God's servant and messenger. His prayer, fasting, and prostration were the natural acts of a created being before his Creator, and his instruction 'so worship Him' places his followers on the same path he walked. On this reading, nothing in his prayer life needs explaining; it is simply what a prophet does.

Mainstream Christianity holds — and fairness requires stating this properly — that Jesus is the eternal Son of God who took on a complete human nature. Christians do not believe in three gods; the doctrine of the Trinity teaches one God in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. When Jesus prays, Christian theology says, the incarnate Son prays in and through his human nature, and his prayer expresses the eternal loving communion between the Son and the Father. On this reading, Jesus praying to the Father is not one god petitioning another, and not God talking to himself, but a real relationship between distinct persons within the one God.

The texts say: Jesus rose before dawn to pray, withdrew habitually to pray, prayed all night 'to God,' fell on his face and yielded his will to the Father's, offered petitions 'with strong crying and tears to him who was able to save him from death,' was 'heard for his godly fear,' addressed the Father as 'the only true God,' and taught that God alone is to be worshiped and served. The Quran says he called himself the servant of Allah, was commanded to pray and give charity for as long as he lived, and told everyone that God is 'my Lord and your Lord.'

The Islamic argument at this point is an argument from simplicity. Both readings are internally coherent. But one requires interpretive machinery the texts themselves never state — two natures in one person, prayer offered by one divine person to another — while the other requires nothing beyond the plain sentences. Read at face value, the man these passages describe is a worshiper of God. That is the entire Islamic claim, and it is the Quran's claim in Jesus' own voice.

sections.title: Clearing Up Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
Misconception one: 'Christians believe Jesus prayed to himself.' This is not what Christian theology teaches, and repeating it attacks a position no informed Christian holds. Trinitarian doctrine distinguishes the persons of the Father and the Son, so in Christian terms the Son prays to the Father, not to himself. The serious Islamic critique is not that the doctrine is self-evidently absurd, but that it is a framework added onto texts that read naturally without it.

Misconception two: 'Muslims deny or diminish Jesus.' The opposite is true. The Quran calls Jesus the Messiah, a word from God, strengthened by the Holy Spirit, born of a virgin, and a worker of miracles by God's permission. A Muslim's faith is not valid without believing in him. What Islam denies is one thing only: that he is God or the literal son of God.

Misconception three: 'Jesus prayed only as an example for others, not because he needed to.' The texts resist this. Mark 1:35 and Luke 5:16 describe private prayer, away from every observer, before dawn and in deserted places — the opposite of a teaching demonstration. And Hebrews 5:7 describes petitions offered 'with strong crying and tears' to the One able to save him. That is real need, not theater.

Misconception four: 'Calling Jesus a servant of God is an insult.' In the Bible's own vocabulary, 'servant of God' is a title of honor carried by the prophets, and the Quran uses the equivalent word, abd, for its most honored figures, including the Prophet Muhammad himself. When the Quran's infant Jesus says 'I am the servant of Allah,' he is claiming a rank, not accepting a demotion.

sections.title: The Straight Path of the Praying Messiah
sections.body:
Stand back from the details and look at the shape of the life the sources describe. Here is a man who rises in the dark to pray while his companions sleep. He fasts. He withdraws to deserts and mountains to be alone with God. He spends entire nights in prayer. On the worst night of his life he does not proclaim his own power; he falls on his face in the dust and says, in effect: Your will, not mine. He calls the Father 'the only true God.' He answers the devil with scripture: worship God, serve him only.

Islam asks one simple question: whom does that life describe — the One who is worshiped, or one who worships?

The Quran's answer is that this is the life of a beloved servant and mighty messenger of God: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah.' Nothing in the Gospel record of his devotion has to be reinterpreted or qualified for that answer to work. And Jesus' own recorded instruction — worship the Lord your God and serve him only; Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him — is precisely what a Muslim does five times a day, with the same prostration Jesus made in Gethsemane.

To honor Jesus, on the Islamic view, is not to worship him but to worship as he worshiped and whom he worshiped. That, the Quran says twice in his own voice, is 'a straight path' — the sirat al-mustaqim from which this library takes its name. The invitation of Islam is simply this: follow the praying Messiah to the God he prayed to.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: If Jesus is God, why did he pray?
A: Christians answer that the eternal Son took on a real human nature, and in that nature he genuinely prayed, depended, and submitted; his prayer also expresses the eternal relationship between the Son and the Father. Muslims answer that a far simpler explanation is the one Jesus gives in the Quran and lives out in the Gospels: he prayed because God was his God and Lord.

Q: Do Christians believe Jesus prayed to himself?
A: No. Trinitarian teaching distinguishes the persons of the Father and the Son within the one God, so the Son prays to the Father. Muslims find this framework unnecessary, but it should be described accurately.

Q: Did Jesus pray the way Muslims pray?
A: There are striking overlaps: Matthew 26:39 shows him prostrate with his face to the ground, he fasted, and he prayed through the night and before dawn. Islam does not claim his ritual was identical to today's salah, only that his posture before God — servant before Lord — is the same.

Q: Doesn't John 17 support the Trinity, since Jesus asks the Father to glorify the Son?
A: Christians do read it that way, and that reading should be engaged rather than dismissed. Muslims reply that in the very same prayer Jesus calls the Father 'the only true God' and defines himself as the one God 'sent' — the vocabulary of a messenger, which is exactly the Quran's category for him.

Q: Is 'servant of God' a demotion of Jesus?
A: Not in either scripture's vocabulary. The Bible gives the title to its greatest prophets, and in the Quran servanthood to God is the highest human station, applied to Muhammad as well. Jesus claiming it from the cradle is presented as an honor, not an insult.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below are offered for general further reading only. They are not cited as proof of any specific claim in this article; the argument above rests on the scripture passages quoted, which were retrieved word for word from the Quran (Saheeh International translation) and the World English Bible.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature. A scholarly collection showing how deeply Jesus is loved and honored in the Islamic tradition.

Bart D. Ehrman — How Jesus Became God. A historical study by a non-Muslim scholar of how beliefs about Jesus' divinity developed in early Christianity.

C. S. Lewis — Mere Christianity. A classic, accessible presentation of mainstream Christian belief, useful for understanding the Christian position in its own voice.

Muhammad 'Ata ur-Rahim — Jesus, Prophet of Islam. An Islamic perspective on the history of beliefs about Jesus.

citations.title: The Quran — Tanzil Uthmani text with Saheeh International translation (served via api.alquran.cloud)
citations.note: Arabic text and translation fetched programmatically per verse; verify spot-checks against a printed mushaf and the published Saheeh International edition before final publication.

citations.title: The Holy Bible, World English Bible (public domain)
citations.note: Verse text fetched programmatically via bible-api.com; public-domain translation suitable for quotation.

relatedArticles.title: Who Is Jesus?
relatedArticles.subtitle: Isa ibn Maryam in Islam and Christ in Christianity — two answers to history's most important question
relatedArticles.summary: Jesus of Nazareth — called Isa ibn Maryam (Jesus, son of Mary) in the Quran — is deeply honored by both Islam and Christianity, yet the two faiths answer the question 'Who is he?' very differently. Islam teaches that Jesus (peace be upon him) was born of the Virgin Mary by a miracle, that he is the Messiah and one of God's greatest messengers, and that he performed miracles by God's permission — but that he is neither God nor the son of God. Mainstream Christianity teaches that Jesus is the second person of the Trinity: the eternal Word of God who became human, was crucified for humanity's sins, and rose again. This article presents both positions from their own sources and then compares them against the quoted texts themselves.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Jesus of Nazareth is loved and honored by more people than any other figure in history — by roughly two billion Christians and roughly two billion Muslims. But the two faiths give different answers to the question of who he actually is.

Islam teaches that Jesus — known in Arabic as Isa ibn Maryam, 'Jesus, son of Mary' (peace be upon him) — was born of the Virgin Mary by a miracle, that he is the Messiah promised to Israel, a mighty messenger of God who performed miracles by God's permission, and a man who will be honored in this life and the next. Islam also teaches, just as firmly, that he is not God and not the son of God.

Mainstream Christianity teaches that Jesus is the second person of the Trinity — one God existing in three persons — the eternal Word of God who took on human nature, was crucified for humanity's sins, and rose from the dead.

This article presents each position as its own scholars state it, then places the quoted texts side by side so you can weigh them for yourself.

sections.title: Introduction: A Question Both Faiths Care About
sections.body:
No conversation between a Muslim and a Christian gets very far before it arrives at Jesus. That is not an accident. For Christians, Jesus is the center of the faith itself. For Muslims, belief in Jesus is not optional either: the Quran names him dozens of times, devotes an entire chapter to his mother Mary (Surah Maryam, the nineteenth chapter of the Quran), and makes belief in him one of the required articles of faith. A person cannot be a Muslim while rejecting Jesus.

This surprises many readers meeting Islam for the first time. Islam is, in a real sense, a Jesus-affirming religion. It affirms his miraculous virgin birth, his title of Messiah (al-Masih in Arabic — the anointed one promised to the Children of Israel), his miracles, and his honored rank among the greatest messengers God ever sent.

Where the two faiths part ways is on one precise point: is Jesus divine? Christianity's mainstream answer, settled in its creeds, is yes — he is God the Son, fully divine and fully human. Islam's answer is no — he is God's servant and messenger, fully human and nothing less than magnificent in that role.

Because the disagreement is precise, it can be examined honestly. In what follows, every verse of the Quran and the Bible is quoted directly from its source text, so that nothing rests on anyone's memory or paraphrase.

sections.title: The Real Question: Not Whether Jesus Matters, but Who He Is
sections.body:
It helps to clear away what is not in dispute. Islam and Christianity agree that Jesus existed, that he was born of the Virgin Mary without a human father, that he is the Messiah, that he performed genuine miracles, that he taught people to worship God, and that his story is not over — both traditions expect his return before the end of history.

That is a remarkable amount of common ground. The disagreement sits on top of it, and it can be stated in one sentence. Christianity says Jesus is the Creator who entered His creation; Islam says Jesus is the greatest of a long line of human messengers sent by the Creator.

Everything else — the Trinity, the crucifixion as atonement for sin, the meaning of the title 'Son of God' — flows from that single fork in the road.

So the fair way to proceed is to ask three questions in order. What does the Quran say Jesus is? What does the Bible say Jesus is, as mainstream Christianity reads it? And when the two sets of texts are placed side by side, which portrait do they most naturally support? The sections below take those questions one at a time.

sections.title: Historical Context: From Roman Judea to the Creeds
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century, in Judea and Galilee, lands then under Roman rule. The Judaism of his time is known to historians as Second Temple Judaism — the period when the rebuilt Temple still stood in Jerusalem — and its heartbeat was uncompromising monotheism. The daily Jewish confession, the Shema, declared that the Lord is one, and Jesus himself is recorded in Mark's Gospel reciting exactly that confession (we will quote it below). Whatever else the first hearers of Jesus understood him to be, they heard him inside that world: a Jewish teacher, preaching to Jews, about the one God of Israel.

After Jesus' time on earth ended, his followers spread across the Roman world, and a long debate began over how to describe him. Historians of doctrine document that the precise formulations Christians now recite took shape gradually over the first several centuries, as church thinkers with differing views — some holding Jesus to be subordinate to the Father, others insisting on his full deity — argued their cases.

A decisive moment came in the year 325, when the emperor Constantine hosted a council of bishops at Nicaea. The council produced the original Nicene Creed, declaring that the Father and the Son are homoousios — Greek for 'one in essence' or 'consubstantial' — and it condemned the position of Arius, a priest who taught that the Son was a created being. Later councils, including those of 431 and 451, continued to refine the doctrine, and classical Christian teaching came to affirm that Christ is one person with both a divine and a human nature. (The dates and outcomes above are confirmed by the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's history of Trinitarian doctrine.)

Christians understand this history as the church clarifying, under God's guidance, what was implicit in Jesus' words all along. Muslims read the same history differently: as evidence that the doctrine of Jesus' deity was a later development, moving step by step away from the simple monotheism Jesus preached in Galilee. The historical facts are shared; the interpretation is the debate.

sections.title: Jesus in the Quran: Servant, Messiah, Miracle
sections.body:
The Quran's account of Jesus begins with an angelic announcement to Mary:

إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ يَٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍۢ مِّنْهُ ٱسْمُهُ ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ وَجِيهًۭا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْءَاخِرَةِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ

"[And mention] when the angels said, 'O Mary, indeed Allah gives you good tidings of a word from Him, whose name will be the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary - distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near [to Allah]." (Quran 3:45)

Notice the titles packed into one verse: Messiah, son of Mary, honored in both worlds. The announcement continues:

وَيُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًۭا وَمِنَ ٱلصَّٰلِحِينَ

"He will speak to the people in the cradle and in maturity and will be of the righteous.'" (Quran 3:46)

Mary, unmarried and chaste, asks the obvious question — and receives the Quran's explanation of the virgin birth:

قَالَتْ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى وَلَدٌۭ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌۭ ۖ قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًۭا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

"She said, 'My Lord, how will I have a child when no man has touched me?' [The angel] said, 'Such is Allah; He creates what He wills. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, "Be," and it is." (Quran 3:47)

The virgin birth, in the Quran, is a creative miracle — like the creation of Adam, who had neither father nor mother — not evidence of divine sonship. God says 'Be,' and it is.

In Surah Maryam, the infant Jesus speaks from the cradle, and his very first recorded words define him:

قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا

"[Jesus] said, 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet." (Quran 19:30)

وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّۭا

"And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive" (Quran 19:31)

وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَتِى وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِى جَبَّارًۭا شَقِيًّۭا

"And [made me] dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a wretched tyrant." (Quran 19:32)

وَٱلسَّلَٰمُ عَلَىَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدتُّ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّۭا

"And peace is on me the day I was born and the day I will die and the day I am raised alive.'" (Quran 19:33)

Servant of God, prophet, blessed, a man of prayer and charity, devoted to his mother: this is the Quran's Jesus in his own miraculous words.

The Quran also affirms his miracles and divine support:

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَقَفَّيْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ بِٱلرُّسُلِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ وَأَيَّدْنَٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ ۗ أَفَكُلَّمَا جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُكُمُ ٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ فَفَرِيقًۭا كَذَّبْتُمْ وَفَرِيقًۭا تَقْتُلُونَ

"And We did certainly give Moses the Torah and followed up after him with messengers. And We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Pure Spirit. But is it [not] that every time a messenger came to you, [O Children of Israel], with what your souls did not desire, you were arrogant? And a party [of messengers] you denied and another party you killed." (Quran 2:87)

Jesus stands here in a chain of messengers reaching back through Moses. And the Quran states his human nature with disarming simplicity:

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌۭ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌۭ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

"The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded." (Quran 5:75)

'They both used to eat food' — a quiet, powerful argument. One who eats is a creature with needs; the Creator needs nothing.

Finally, the Quran addresses Christians directly, with respect but without ambiguity:

يَٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌۭ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًۭا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۖ سُبْحَٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًۭا

"O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, 'Three'; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs." (Quran 4:171)

The verse honors Jesus with three titles — messenger of God, His word, a soul from Him — and in the same breath draws the line: do not commit excess; God is one; He has no son.

sections.title: Jesus in the Bible: The Christian Reading and the Texts
sections.body:
Fairness requires stating the Christian position as Christians themselves state it. Mainstream Christianity — Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant alike — teaches that Jesus is the eternal Word of God who took on human nature: one person, fully divine and fully human. This is monotheism as Christians define it: not three gods, but one God existing in three persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. In support, Christian scholars point especially to the opening of John's Gospel, which describes God's eternal Word taking on flesh, to passages where Jesus forgives sins and accepts worship, and to the early church's practice of praying to Christ. On the classical Christian reading, Jesus' human limitations belong to his human nature, while his divine nature remained fully God.

That is the doctrine. Now let us hear the texts themselves, quoted from the World English Bible translation. In John's Gospel, Jesus prays to the Father the night before his arrest:

"This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ." (John 17:3)

Here Jesus calls the Father 'the only true God' and describes himself as the one 'whom you sent' — the very definition of a messenger.

When asked which commandment is greatest, Jesus answers with Israel's ancient confession of God's oneness:

"Jesus answered, 'The greatest is, "Hear, Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one:"'" (Mark 12:29)

After the resurrection accounts, John records Jesus saying to Mary Magdalene:

"Jesus said to her, 'Don't hold me, for I haven't yet ascended to my Father; but go to my brothers, and tell them, "I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God."'" (John 20:17)

Jesus speaks of 'my God' — he has a God whom he worships, the same God his followers worship.

When a man addresses him as 'Good teacher,' Jesus deflects the compliment toward God:

"Behold, one came to him and said, 'Good teacher, what good thing shall I do, that I may have eternal life?' He said to him, 'Why do you call me good? No one is good but one, that is, God. But if you want to enter into life, keep the commandments.'" (Matthew 19:16-17)

Jesus also describes the source of his authority in terms of dependence and obedience:

"I can of myself do nothing. As I hear, I judge, and my judgment is righteous; because I don't seek my own will, but the will of my Father who sent me." (John 5:30)

In the Acts of the Apostles, Peter — Jesus' chief disciple — introduces Jesus to a Jerusalem crowd in these words:

"'Men of Israel, hear these words! Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved by God to you by mighty works and wonders and signs which God did by him among you, even as you yourselves know,'" (Acts 2:22)

A man approved by God, whose miracles were things 'God did by him' — this is how the earliest recorded sermon describes Jesus.

And in a letter attributed to Paul, the distinction between God and Jesus is stated as a formula:

"For there is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus," (1 Timothy 2:5)

Christian interpreters have readings of each of these passages — most commonly, that Jesus speaks here from his human nature, or in his role as the sent one, without denying his divine nature. That interpretive framework is genuinely held and should be represented honestly. The question the reader must weigh is whether that framework arises from these texts or is brought to them.

sections.title: Side by Side: The Two Portraits and the Texts
sections.body:
