relatedArticles.summary: The Quran was preserved through two simultaneous channels: mass memorization by the Prophet Muhammad's companions and written records made by his scribes during his lifetime. A verified complete collection was assembled under Abu Bakr within about two years of the Prophet's death, and the third caliph, Uthman, distributed standardized copies about two decades later. Since then, unbroken chains of memorizers and manuscripts have carried the text in numbers Muslim scholars call mutawatir — too many independent transmitters for coordinated error. This article walks through that history, the Quran's own preservation promise, and an honest explanation of the canonical reading traditions (qira'at).

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body: The Quran was preserved through two channels working at once: living memorization and writing. During the Prophet Muhammad's lifetime his companions memorized the revelation as it came down while his scribes wrote it out; within about two years of his death the written records were gathered into one verified collection, and roughly two decades later the third caliph, Uthman, sent standardized copies to the major Muslim cities. Every generation since has passed the text on through both memory and manuscript in numbers Muslim scholars call mutawatir — too many independent carriers for coordinated error. The Quran frames this as a divine promise: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." (Quran 15:9).

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
Ask an honest question of any scripture and you are really asking two questions at once. First: what did the original message say? Second: can I trust that the text in my hands today still says it? For the Quran, Muslims give an unusually confident answer to that second question, and this article explains why in plain language.

We will look at three things: the history of how the Quran was memorized, written, collected, and standardized; the Quran's own words about its preservation; and the qira'at — the canonical reading traditions — explained honestly, because a fair account should address them rather than hide them.

Two terms before we begin. A hafiz (plural: huffaz) is a person who has memorized the entire Quran word for word. Tawatur means mass transmission: a report carried independently by so many people in every generation that they could not all have agreed on a lie or made the same mistake.

sections.title: The Question We Are Answering
sections.body:
The precise question is this: by what mechanism did the words recited by the Prophet Muhammad in seventh-century Arabia reach the printed copy on a shelf today, and how strong is the evidence that nothing was lost or changed along the way?

Notice what the question is not. It is not whether the Quran is true or divine — those are separate questions with their own articles. It is a question about transmission: the pipeline between the original recitation and the modern page. The Quran's pipeline has an unusual structure: two independent channels — mass memorization and writing — running side by side from the first generation, each checking the other.

sections.title: From Recitation to Standard Copies: The History
sections.body:
The Quran was revealed piecemeal over about twenty-three years, from roughly 610 CE until the Prophet Muhammad's death in 632 CE. It did not arrive as a bound book but as a recitation — the word Quran itself means "recitation" — and from the beginning the community received it by hearing and repeating it.

Seventh-century Arabia had a powerful memorization culture, and the Quran entered it as its most treasured text. The Prophet's companions memorized the revelation as it came, and because Muslims recite the Quran aloud in daily congregational prayers, the text was continuously performed in public — a slip before listeners who know it by heart is corrected on the spot. Hadith collections such as Sahih al-Bukhari also relate that the Prophet reviewed the revelation with the angel Gabriel every Ramadan, and twice in his final year.

Memory was not the only channel. The Prophet appointed scribes — Zayd ibn Thabit is the best known — who wrote each passage down as it was revealed, on parchment, palm stalks, flat stones, and shoulder bones, with the Prophet directing where each passage belonged within its surah (chapter). So by 632 CE the entire Quran existed twice over: in the memories of the huffaz and in written records.

The first gathering into a single collection came almost immediately. According to the account preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari, after the Battle of Yamama killed a number of memorizers, Umar ibn al-Khattab urged the first caliph, Abu Bakr, to have the written records collected. Abu Bakr commissioned Zayd ibn Thabit, who verified each verse against both the written records and the testimony of those who had learned it directly from the Prophet. The collection passed from Abu Bakr to Umar and then to Umar's daughter Hafsa, a widow of the Prophet.

The final step came under the third caliph, Uthman ibn Affan, around two decades after the Prophet's death. As Islam spread beyond Arabia, disputes arose over styles of recitation. Uthman had authoritative copies made from the collection kept with Hafsa, sent one to each major city, and ordered divergent private copies destroyed — a public policy to unify recitation, reported openly in Muslim sources, which also record that the companions broadly supported it. The standardized text is called the Uthmanic mushaf (a mushaf is a physical copy of the Quran), and its consonantal skeleton underlies every Quran printed today.

Physical evidence from that first century survives. Among the earliest is a set of parchment folios in the Mingana Collection at the University of Birmingham, containing parts of surahs 18, 19, and 20 in the early Arabic script known as Hijazi. According to the university, radiocarbon testing at Oxford dated the parchment to between 568 and 645 CE with 95.4 percent probability — a range overlapping the Prophet's own lifetime. Anyone can set those passages beside a modern copy and compare.

sections.title: The Quran's Own Promise of Preservation
sections.body:
Muslims read this history through a promise the Quran makes about itself. In Surah al-Hijr, God declares:

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَٰفِظُونَ

"Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." (Quran 15:9)

The claim is striking: the Quran does not ask its community to preserve it as a duty they might fail; it announces that God Himself has taken responsibility for guarding it. Muslims understand the whole history above — memorizers, scribes, early collection, standardized copies — as the means by which that promise was carried out.

The same theme appears at the very moment of revelation. Surah al-Qiyamah addresses the Prophet as he received the words, moving his tongue hurriedly for fear of losing them:

إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُۥ وَقُرْءَانَهُۥ

"Indeed, upon Us is its collection [in your heart] and [to make possible] its recitation." (Quran 75:17)

فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَٰهُ فَٱتَّبِعْ قُرْءَانَهُۥ

"So when We have recited it [through Gabriel], then follow its recitation." (Quran 75:18)

From the first, then, retention was declared God's responsibility rather than a human hope.

Two further passages describe the Quran's guarded status. In Surah al-Waqi'ah:

إِنَّهُۥ لَقُرْءَانٌۭ كَرِيمٌۭ

"Indeed, it is a noble Qur'an" (Quran 56:77)

فِى كِتَٰبٍۢ مَّكْنُونٍۢ

"In a Register well-protected;" (Quran 56:78)

And in Surah al-Buruj:

بَلْ هُوَ قُرْءَانٌۭ مَّجِيدٌۭ

"But this is an honored Qur'an" (Quran 85:21)

فِى لَوْحٍۢ مَّحْفُوظٍۭ

"[Inscribed] in a Preserved Slate." (Quran 85:22)

Muslim commentators have generally understood the "Register well-protected" and the "Preserved Slate" (in Arabic, al-lawh al-mahfuz) as the heavenly original kept with God: the Quran's source is guarded above, and its earthly text is guarded below.

A scripture's claim about itself cannot, by itself, prove that claim — a skeptic may fairly say so. That is why this article began with history rather than theology. The Muslim case is that the promise and the record fit together: the Quran announced it would be guarded, and the transmission history is what that guarding looked like in practice.

sections.title: What Are the Qira'at? The Reading Traditions Explained Honestly
sections.body:
Anyone who researches the Quran online will quickly meet the word qira'at, sometimes presented as a hidden problem. It is not hidden, and understanding it honestly is part of understanding preservation. (Because no Bible passages are required for this topic, this section stands in place of the usual biblical-perspective section.)

A qira'ah (plural: qira'at) is a canonical reading tradition — a complete, documented way of reciting the text, transmitted from the Prophet through his companions and the named master reciters of the early generations. Ten readings are recognized as canonical, each named after a master reciter and carried by his students. The most widespread today is the reading of Asim of Kufa as transmitted by Hafs; the reading of Nafi of Medina as transmitted by Warsh is common in North and West Africa. A printed Quran follows one of these readings and identifies which.

Most differences between readings are matters of pronunciation: vowel length, articulation, dialectal features of early Arabic. A smaller number involve a different grammatical form or word — transmitted as complementary, not contradictory. The best-known example is the fourth verse of the opening chapter. In the reading of Hafs it is:

مَٰلِكِ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ

"Sovereign of the Day of Recompense." (Quran 1:4)

Other canonical readings recite the first word as malik, "King," rather than the long maalik, "Master" or "Owner." King of the Day of Judgment and Master of the Day of Judgment are two true descriptions of the same God — a difference in emphasis, not doctrine. This example is typical: no article of Islamic belief or practice differs between canonical readings.

Hadith collections relate that the Quran was revealed in several modes (ahruf) to ease recitation for tribes with different dialects. Scholars have long discussed how exactly the ahruf relate to the qira'at, but the practical criteria are agreed: a reading is canonical only if it has an authentic, unbroken chain to the Prophet, conforms to the Uthmanic written skeleton, and is sound Arabic. Readings that failed these tests were set aside by Muslim scholars themselves, openly.

The honest summary: the qira'at are not textual corruption uncovered by critics. They are a documented canon of recitation, preserved with the same chains and rigor as the text itself, and written about openly since Islam's earliest centuries.

sections.title: How This Compares with Other Ancient Texts
sections.body:
Most works from the ancient world survive through one channel only: manuscript copying. Scribes copied manuscripts, later scribes copied the copies, and scholars today reconstruct the earliest recoverable wording by comparing surviving copies — a discipline called textual criticism. That is not an insult to any tradition; it is simply how texts without a living memorization institution travel through time.

The New Testament, for example, is described by Christian textual scholars themselves as a manuscript tradition: the original autographs have not survived, thousands of later Greek manuscripts exist with variations among them, and modern critical editions are careful reconstructions of the earliest attainable text. Mainstream Christian scholarship states this plainly and regards most variants as minor; the companion article on how the Bible was transmitted tells that story fairly.

The Quran's transmission has a different structure. It has two channels rather than one — complete memorization and writing, each correcting the other from the first generation. Standardization happened within about two decades of the source rather than emerging from centuries of copying. And the oral channel is institutional and continuous: every generation has produced huffaz in large numbers, certified in unbroken teacher-to-student chains, with the text performed daily in public worship where errors are caught immediately.

None of this, by itself, settles which scripture is true. What it establishes is narrower and important: the claim that the Quran read today is the Quran of the seventh century rests on an unusually strong transmission mechanism, and readers can weigh that evidence for themselves.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few misunderstandings come up so often that they deserve direct answers.

"Uthman created his own version of the Quran and burned the evidence." Uthman did not compose or edit a text; he had copies made from the collection compiled under Abu Bakr — assembled by the Prophet's own scribe under strict verification — and distributed them. The destruction of divergent private copies was a public, announced policy to end recitation disputes, supported by the companions and reported openly in Muslim sources — which is not how cover-ups behave.

"The qira'at prove there are many different Qurans." The qira'at are canonical reading traditions with documented chains, differing mostly in pronunciation, with no difference in doctrine. Muslim scholars catalogued them centuries ago; they are a studied feature of the tradition, not a leak.

"The Quran was only written down centuries after Muhammad." Writing began during the Prophet's lifetime; a complete verified collection existed within about two years of his death; standard copies circulated within about two decades. The Birmingham folios described earlier were radiocarbon dated, per the University of Birmingham, to a range overlapping the Prophet's lifetime.

"Early Qurans had no dots or vowel marks, so the text must have changed when marks were added." Early Arabic script recorded a consonantal skeleton (the rasm), and readers supplied pronunciation from the oral tradition — the primary channel all along. The dots and vowel signs added later did not create a new text; they encoded in ink the recitation the community already carried by heart.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Put the pieces side by side. A promise: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." (Quran 15:9). A mechanism: memorization and writing in parallel from the first generation, a verified collection within about two years of the Prophet's death, standardized copies within about two decades, and unbroken teacher-to-student chains ever since. And a result anyone can test: a hafiz from Indonesia and a hafiz from Senegal who learned the same canonical reading will recite the entire book identically.

For Muslims this is not a fortunate accident; it is what a fulfilled divine promise looks like. The Quran describes itself as "Indeed, it is a noble Qur'an" "In a Register well-protected;" (Quran 56:77-78) — guarded at its source with God and, Muslims hold, guarded in its earthly text among people.

So here is an invitation rather than a demand. If you are weighing Islam's claims, start where the evidence is most concrete and checkable. Read the text, listen to a memorizer recite it, and compare a seventh-century folio with a modern page. A serious search for God deserves a text you can trust — and the Quran's case for that trust is open for inspection.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: What does hafiz mean, and how many huffaz are there today?
A: A hafiz is a person who has memorized the entire Quran word for word. There is no official global census, but memorization schools operate on every continent and complete memorizers are commonly numbered in the millions. The text has never depended on a few individuals.

Q: What is tawatur?
A: Mass transmission: a report carried independently by so many people in every generation that coordinated fabrication or shared error is practically impossible. Muslim scholars hold that the Quran's text is mutawatir, transmitted this way both orally and in writing.

Q: Are the qira'at different Qurans?
A: No. They are canonical reading traditions transmitted with documented chains, differing mostly in pronunciation and occasionally in complementary word forms. No Islamic belief or practice differs between canonical readings.

Q: What is the Birmingham Quran manuscript?
A: Parchment folios at the University of Birmingham containing parts of surahs 18, 19, and 20 in early Hijazi script. Per the university, radiocarbon testing dated the parchment to 568-645 CE with 95.4 percent probability — a range overlapping the Prophet's lifetime.

Q: Did Uthman change the Quran?
A: No. He commissioned copies of the collection already compiled under Abu Bakr by the Prophet's scribe Zayd ibn Thabit and sent them to the major cities to end disputes over recitation. Muslim sources report the episode openly and record broad support from the companions.

Q: Does the Quran itself claim it will be preserved?
A: Yes. Quran 15:9 states: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." Muslims understand the memorizers, manuscripts, and unbroken chains of history as the fulfillment of that promise.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
Muhammad Mustafa al-Azami's The History of the Qur'anic Text is a detailed scholarly study of the Quran's collection and transmission. Ingrid Mattson's The Story of the Qur'an is an accessible academic overview of how the Quran was revealed, compiled, and lived with. Ahmad von Denffer's Ulum al-Qur'an is a beginner-friendly manual of the classical Quranic sciences, including the qira'at. Michael Sells's Approaching the Qur'an introduces the sound of the recited Quran to readers with no Arabic.

For the manuscript evidence discussed above, the University of Birmingham's Cadbury Research Library publishes information about its Quran folios, including the radiocarbon dating, on its official website.

relatedArticles.title: How Was the Bible Transmitted?
relatedArticles.subtitle: From spoken word to Greek manuscripts to modern translations — the journey of the Christian scriptures, told honestly
relatedArticles.summary: The Bible reached today's readers through a long historical journey: oral teaching, composition in Greek, hand copying for many centuries, gradual agreement on the canon, and chains of translation. This article presents that history as mainstream Christian scholarship itself describes it, using Luke's own prologue as a window into how the Gospels were composed. It then offers the Islamic perspective: the Quran affirms the original Gospel of Jesus as guidance and light, while warning that scripture can be altered by human hands. The aim is an honest, respectful account that helps seekers compare the two transmission stories fairly.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
The Bible did not arrive as a single finished book. It grew over centuries: Jesus taught orally, authors later wrote accounts in Greek, scribes copied those writings by hand for well over a thousand years, and church leaders gradually agreed on which books belonged.

Almost everything in this article comes from Christian scholarship itself — the discipline called textual criticism, built largely by devout Christians. Islam, for its part, honors the original Gospel given to Jesus as true revelation from God. Knowing how the Bible reached us helps Christians and Muslims discuss scripture honestly, without caricature on either side.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
Every scripture has a history — a real, human journey from the moment of revelation to the printed page in your hands. This article traces that journey for the Bible, especially the New Testament.

A word about method. Nothing here relies on polemics. The account below is the standard picture taught in Christian seminaries. The discipline that studies it, textual criticism, was developed overwhelmingly by Christian scholars who loved the Bible and wanted to recover its earliest wording. When modern Christian Bibles carry footnotes noting that some ancient manuscripts lack a verse, that honesty is the fruit of their work.

Islam enters this conversation with respect. The Quran speaks of the Injil — the Gospel given to Jesus — as genuine revelation, "guidance and light." The Islamic question is not whether God spoke through Jesus; Muslims affirm that He did. The question is what happened to that message afterward.

sections.title: The Question We Are Asking
sections.body:
What happened between Jesus teaching in Galilee and the Bible on a bookshelf today?

Break it into stages. First, an oral stage: Jesus wrote no book, and his teaching circulated by word of mouth. Second, a writing stage: letters and Gospel accounts were composed in Greek in the decades after his ministry. Third, a copying stage: before printing, every copy was made by hand. Fourth, a canon stage: communities gradually agreed on which writings counted as scripture. Fifth, a translation stage: the text passed through Greek and Latin into thousands of languages.

Each stage is described by Christian historians themselves.

sections.title: From Spoken Word to Printed Page
sections.body:
Begin with language. Historians broadly agree that Jesus taught in Aramaic, the everyday tongue of first-century Jewish Galilee, yet the New Testament was written in Greek. Apart from a few preserved Aramaic phrases, the recorded words of Jesus therefore already stand in translation — a point made routinely in Christian introductions to the New Testament.

The earliest Christian writings are generally held to be the letters of Paul; the four Gospels came later in the first century, according to mainstream dating, drawing on oral tradition and earlier written material. None of the original documents — the autographs — survives for any book of the Bible. What survives are copies of copies.

Hand copying introduces variation. The surviving Greek manuscripts of the New Testament number in the thousands, from small early papyrus fragments — the earliest commonly dated to the second century — to complete codices such as Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, generally dated to the fourth century. Comparing them reveals many textual variants. Mainstream textual critics, most of them Christians, describe the great majority as trivial: spelling, word order, slips of the pen. A smaller number are significant, and modern Christian Bibles mark them openly — well-known examples include the longer ending of Mark and the story of the woman caught in adultery in John, both flagged in standard translations as absent from the earliest manuscripts.

The canon — the official list of biblical books — took shape gradually. Early communities used differing collections, and the earliest surviving list matching exactly today's twenty-seven New Testament books appears in a letter of Athanasius, bishop of Alexandria, in the later fourth century. Even now the Old Testament canon differs among Christians: Catholic and Orthodox Bibles include books, called deuterocanonical, that Protestant Bibles omit.

Finally, translation. The Hebrew scriptures were rendered into Greek — the Septuagint — before Jesus's time, and New Testament authors often quote that version. Jerome's Latin Vulgate later served the Western church for roughly a thousand years. Modern translations work from critical editions: scholarly reconstructions assembled by comparing manuscripts. All of this is the mainstream account, told by Christian scholarship with admirable honesty.

sections.title: What the Quran Says About the Gospel
sections.body:
The Quran's starting point is reverence for what God gave Jesus. In Surah al-Ma'idah, God says:

وَقَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَٰرِهِم بِعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ مُصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَٰهُ ٱلْإِنجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدًۭى وَنُورٌۭ وَمُصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَهُدًۭى وَمَوْعِظَةًۭ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ

"And We sent, following in their footsteps, Jesus, the son of Mary, confirming that which came before him in the Torah; and We gave him the Gospel, in which was guidance and light and confirming that which preceded it of the Torah as guidance and instruction for the righteous." (Quran 5:46)

The Injil — the Gospel given to Jesus — is described as genuine revelation: guidance and light, confirming the Torah before it. Islam affirms the message of Jesus as coming from the same God who sent the Quran.

The very next verse addresses Christians directly:

وَلْيَحْكُمْ أَهْلُ ٱلْإِنجِيلِ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَٰسِقُونَ

"And let the People of the Gospel judge by what Allah has revealed therein. And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed - then it is those who are the defiantly disobedient." (Quran 5:47)

Christians are called the People of the Gospel and urged to judge by what God revealed in it — the Gospel's original content is treated as authoritative divine guidance.

At the same time, the Quran warns that revelation can be interfered with by human hands:

فَوَيْلٌۭ لِّلَّذِينَ يَكْتُبُونَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ يَقُولُونَ هَٰذَا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَشْتَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًۭا قَلِيلًۭا ۖ فَوَيْلٌۭ لَّهُم مِّمَّا كَتَبَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَوَيْلٌۭ لَّهُم مِّمَّا يَكْسِبُونَ

"So woe to those who write the "scripture" with their own hands, then say, "This is from Allah," in order to exchange it for a small price. Woe to them for what their hands have written and woe to them for what they earn." (Quran 2:79)

This is the concept Muslims call tahrif: distortion of scripture. Two nuances matter. First, classical Muslim scholars differed over its extent — some emphasized alteration of the text, others alteration of meaning while much true text remained. Second, the verse condemns those who wrote material and falsely attributed it to God; it is not a blanket charge against every scribe or every Christian.

So the Islamic frame is neither "the Bible is worthless" nor "the Bible is exactly the original revelation." God truly gave Jesus a Gospel of guidance and light, and today's texts reach us through a long human history — carrying truth, alongside the marks of human hands.

sections.title: What the Bible Says About Itself
sections.body:
The New Testament gives us candid windows into its own composition. The clearest is the opening of Luke's Gospel, where the author explains his method:

"Since many have undertaken to set in order a narrative concerning those matters which have been fulfilled among us, even as those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and servants of the word delivered them to us, it seemed good to me also, having traced the course of all things accurately from the first, to write to you in order, most excellent Theophilus; that you might know the certainty concerning the things in which you were instructed." (Luke 1:1-4)

Read that slowly. Luke says many others had already written accounts. He says the material was "delivered" — handed down — from eyewitnesses to people like himself, who was not one. He describes tracing everything "accurately from the first": the language of careful investigation. This is an author describing historical research in his own words; many Christians would say God's inspiration worked precisely through such human effort.

John's Gospel closes with a similar candor about selection:

"There are also many other things which Jesus did, which if they would all be written, I suppose that even the world itself wouldn’t have room for the books that would be written." (John 21:25)

The Gospels, by their own testimony, are selections from a much larger body of things Jesus did and said — chosen and arranged by authors writing for particular audiences.

The classic Christian statement about scripture's nature comes from a letter to Timothy:

"Every Scripture is God-breathed and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for instruction in righteousness," (2 Timothy 3:16)

This verse anchors the Christian doctrine of inspiration: scripture is "God-breathed." It is also fair to note, as many Christian commentators themselves do, that when this letter was written the "Scripture" its first readers knew was above all the Hebrew scriptures — the New Testament as a collection did not yet exist — and that the verse describes scripture's character without listing which books belong.

By the Bible's own account, then, its composition involved prior sources, handed-down testimony, human investigation, and selection. That is where any honest account of transmission must begin.

sections.title: Two Transmission Stories, Side by Side
sections.body:
Set the two transmission stories side by side — each as its own scholarship tells it — and structural differences emerge.

Language: the Quran is preserved and recited in its original Arabic, while the words of Jesus were spoken in Aramaic but preserved in Greek — with few exceptions, not available in his own tongue.

Timescale: Muslim tradition and the early manuscript record describe a complete text standardized within a generation of the Prophet Muhammad's death, while the New Testament canon took shape over centuries.

Mechanism: the Quran's transmission ran on two parallel tracks — mass memorization alongside written copies — each checking the other; the Bible's ran on hand copying alone, which is why variants accumulated and textual criticism became necessary.

Self-description: Luke's prologue describes research and compilation of handed-down reports; the Quran presents itself throughout as the direct speech of God to one messenger.

Two cautions keep this honest. None of these differences impugns anyone's sincerity: generations of Christian scribes labored devotedly, and Christian scholars' candor about variants is a mark of integrity. And the Quran's own history — standardization under the caliph Uthman, early manuscripts, modes of recitation — deserves the same scrutiny, and receives it elsewhere in this library. The narrow point here is that the two scriptures traveled very different roads to reach us.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few misunderstandings cloud this topic on both sides.

"Muslims believe the Bible is worthless." Not so. The Quran calls the original Torah and Gospel guidance and light and calls Christians the People of the Gospel. The Islamic claim concerns transmission, not a denial that God spoke through Jesus.

"Textual variants mean nobody knows what the Bible says." Also not so. Mainstream textual critics hold that the vast majority of variants are minor and the general shape of the text is well established. The serious questions concern specific passages and wording — significant, but not a free-for-all.

"The books of the Bible were chosen at the Council of Nicaea." This story circulates in novels and internet memes, but historians of Christianity describe canon formation as a gradual process reflected in usage, debate, and later lists and councils. Repeating the Nicaea legend weakens any argument built on it.

"Since 2 Timothy says scripture is God-breathed, the transmission question is settled." The verse states a doctrine of inspiration; it does not list the canon's contents, and it could not certify copies and translations made centuries later. Inspiration and transmission are two different questions, and this article is about the second.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion: Continuity and Criterion
sections.body:
Islam's answer to all this is continuity. God did not speak once and fall silent: He sent the Torah to Moses, the Gospel to Jesus, and finally — Muslims believe — the Quran. The Quran describes its own role in that sequence:

وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ وَمُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ ۖ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ عَمَّا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ لِكُلٍّۢ جَعَلْنَا مِنكُمْ شِرْعَةًۭ وَمِنْهَاجًۭا ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةًۭ وَٰحِدَةًۭ وَلَٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِى مَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۖ فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًۭا فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

"And We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], the Book in truth, confirming that which preceded it of the Scripture and as a criterion over it. So judge between them by what Allah has revealed and do not follow their inclinations away from what has come to you of the truth. To each of you We prescribed a law and a method. Had Allah willed, He would have made you one nation [united in religion], but [He intended] to test you in what He has given you; so race to [all that is] good. To Allah is your return all together, and He will [then] inform you concerning that over which you used to differ." (Quran 5:48)

The Quran calls itself a confirmation of earlier scripture and a criterion over it — in Arabic, muhaymin, a guardian and standard. Whatever survives of the earlier revelation is confirmed where it agrees with the Quran, and the Quran serves as the measure where the records differ.

Notice the posture this creates. A Muslim has no need to mock the Bible's history — it is simply what Christian scholarship reports — nor to deny the light that remains in it, which the Quran affirms by name. The invitation is gentler than polemic: if you love the message of Jesus, look at the book that confirms him, honors his mother, and preserves the heart of what he taught — the call to worship the one God alone.

For the Christian reader, this history need not be threatening; your own scholars uncovered it. The open question is where God's guidance is available today in a form whose transmission can be traced with confidence. Islam's answer is the Quran — a claim to be tested against evidence in the companion articles.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Did Jesus write any part of the Bible?
A: No. Mainstream Christian scholarship agrees that Jesus left no writings. The Gospels were written about him, in Greek, in the decades after his ministry — a process Luke describes in his prologue.

Q: Do Muslims believe the Bible is completely corrupted?
A: No. The Quran affirms the original Torah and Gospel as guidance and light, and classical scholars differed over whether the distortion it warns of affected mainly the text or its interpretation. Today's Bible is seen as containing truth alongside material shaped by long human transmission.

Q: What language did Jesus speak, and why does it matter?
A: Historians broadly agree Jesus taught in Aramaic, while the New Testament was composed in Greek. With few exceptions, his words reach us in translation — a fact acknowledged in standard Christian scholarship.

Q: Who decided which books belong in the Bible?
A: No single meeting decided it. Books circulated and were debated for generations; the earliest surviving exact list of today's twenty-seven New Testament books comes from Athanasius in the later fourth century, and Christian communions still differ over the Old Testament canon.

Q: Is textual criticism an attack on Christianity?
A: No. It is a discipline built largely by Christian scholars seeking the earliest recoverable wording of the Bible. The manuscript footnotes in modern Christian Bibles are the fruit of that work.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following works are offered as further reading; listing them does not imply agreement with every conclusion.

Bruce M. Metzger and Bart D. Ehrman — The Text of the New Testament: Its Transmission, Corruption, and Restoration. The standard scholarly introduction to New Testament textual criticism.

Bruce M. Metzger — The Canon of the New Testament: Its Origin, Development, and Significance. A detailed history of how the twenty-seven-book New Testament canon took shape.

F. F. Bruce — The Canon of Scripture. An evangelical Christian scholar's account of canon formation for both Testaments.

Bart D. Ehrman — Misquoting Jesus: The Story Behind Who Changed the Bible and Why. A popular-level introduction to manuscripts and variants; its conclusions are debated among scholars.

Muhammad Mustafa al-Azami — The History of the Qur'anic Text from Revelation to Compilation: A Comparative Study with the Old and New Testaments. A Muslim scholar's comparative study of Quranic and biblical transmission.

A note on sources: Quran quotations were fetched from api.alquran.cloud (Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation) and Bible quotations from bible-api.com (World English Bible), reproduced verbatim. Historical statements are kept to widely established findings, phrased in general terms rather than precise dates.

relatedArticles.title: Who Is God? The Quran and the Bible Compared
relatedArticles.subtitle: What the two scriptures actually say about God's names, oneness, mercy, justice, and nearness to humanity
relatedArticles.summary: The Quran and the Bible both describe one eternal Creator who is merciful, just, all-knowing, and utterly unlike His creation. This article places the two scriptures side by side, quoting each in full, to show where they agree deeply and where they part ways. The agreements — one God, everlasting, merciful and just — are far larger than many people expect. The differences center on whether God ever became human, whether He can be seen, and what 'fatherhood' and 'begetting' language means.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Both the Quran and the Bible teach that there is one God: eternal, the Creator of everything, all-knowing, merciful, and just. On these foundations the two books sound remarkably alike, and this article quotes the actual texts so you can see it.

They differ on three main points. Mainstream Christianity teaches that God exists as a Trinity — one God in three persons — and that the Son became human in Jesus; Islam teaches that God is absolutely one and never becomes part of His creation. The Quran says no vision can grasp God in this life, while Christian theology holds that God made Himself visible in Christ. And the Bible uses 'Father' and 'Son' language that the Quran deliberately avoids, insisting that God 'neither begets nor is born.'

Every verse below is quoted word for word from the Saheeh International translation of the Quran and the World English Bible.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
Ask a Muslim and a Christian to describe God and, for the first several sentences, you may not be able to tell them apart. One God. Creator of the heavens and the earth. Eternal, without beginning or end. Merciful beyond measure, yet perfectly just. Nothing in creation resembles Him.

Then come the sentences where the two descriptions divide — and those differences matter enormously, because they concern the very identity of the One both communities worship.

This article lets each scripture speak for itself: every Quranic verse below is quoted in Arabic with the Saheeh International translation, and every biblical passage from the World English Bible, a public-domain translation of the Hebrew and Greek. We will look at God's names, His oneness, His mercy and justice, His transcendence (a word that simply means God is above and beyond His creation, not limited by it), and His relationship with human beings — and only after both scriptures have been heard fairly will we compare them.

sections.title: The Question We Are Really Asking
sections.body:
The question 'who is God?' breaks into smaller, concrete questions that both scriptures answer directly. Is God one — and in what sense? What is God like: what are His names and attributes (an 'attribute' is simply a quality God has, such as mercy or knowledge)? Is He primarily merciful, primarily just, or both at once? Can He be seen, imagined, or represented? Does He ever take on a created form? And how does He relate to us — as a distant force, a loving parent, a master, a judge?

A fair comparison has to notice something honest at the outset: Muslims and Christians are not arguing about whether there is one eternal Creator. Both firmly say yes. The real disagreement is about what that one God is like and whether He ever entered His own creation. Keeping that distinction clear prevents the most common caricatures on both sides — that Christians worship three gods (they say they do not), or that Muslims worship some other, foreign deity ('Allah' is simply the Arabic word for God).

sections.title: Where These Texts Come From
sections.body:
The Bible is not a single book but a library written over many centuries. The Hebrew Bible (which Christians call the Old Testament) was composed mainly in Hebrew and contains the passages about God's oneness, eternity, and mercy quoted below; Deuteronomy 6:4, known in Judaism as the Shema, remains the central Jewish declaration of faith recited daily. The New Testament was written in Greek in the first century CE by followers of Jesus.

Christian doctrine about God's inner nature developed over the following centuries. The classic formulation of the Trinity — one God in three co-equal persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — was hammered out in church councils, beginning with the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE and continuing through later fourth-century councils. Christians regard this doctrine as the correct interpretation of their scriptures, not an addition to them; that is how their own scholarship describes it, and a fair reader should know that.

The Quran was proclaimed by the Prophet Muhammad in seventh-century Arabia over roughly two decades. It presents itself not as a new religion's founding document but as a restoration of the original message of all prophets — Abraham, Moses, and Jesus included — centered on the absolute oneness of God, called Tawhid in Arabic. Strikingly, the Quran speaks directly to Jews and Christians ('People of the Book'), affirming that their God and its God are the same, while correcting what it presents as later misunderstandings about His nature.

sections.title: God in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran's most concentrated answer to our question is a four-verse chapter, Surah Al-Ikhlaas (chapter 112), which Muslims regard as a summary of the entire doctrine of God. In the printed Quran its first verse is preceded by the basmala — the opening formula 'In the name of God' — which appears at the start of the Arabic below.

بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ

"Say, 'He is Allah, [who is] One," (Quran 112:1)

ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ

"Allah, the Eternal Refuge." (Quran 112:2)

لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ

"He neither begets nor is born," (Quran 112:3)

وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌۢ

"Nor is there to Him any equivalent." (Quran 112:4)

Four claims in forty seconds of recitation: God is one (ahad — a oneness that admits no internal division); He is al-Samad, the self-sufficient One upon whom everything depends while He depends on nothing; He has no parent and no offspring; and nothing whatsoever resembles Him.

The Quran also gives God many names — traditionally counted as ninety-nine — each naming an attribute. A famous passage in Surah Al-Hashr strings a series of them together.

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَٰدَةِ ۖ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, Knower of the unseen and the witnessed. He is the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful." (Quran 59:22)

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

"He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer, the Exalted in Might, the Compeller, the Superior. Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him." (Quran 59:23)

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

"He is Allah, the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner; to Him belong the best names. Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise." (Quran 59:24)

Notice the order: before power and majesty, mercy. 'The Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful' (al-Rahman, al-Rahim) is the description Muslims repeat before almost every action of their day. The Quran invites believers to call on God through all these names.

وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ فَٱدْعُوهُ بِهَا ۖ وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ أَسْمَٰٓئِهِۦ ۚ سَيُجْزَوْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

"And to Allah belong the best names, so invoke Him by them. And leave [the company of] those who practice deviation concerning His names. They will be recompensed for what they have been doing." (Quran 7:180)

At the same time, the Quran guards God's transcendence fiercely. He is nearer to us than our own selves in knowledge and care, yet He cannot be captured by human sight or imagination in this life.

لَّا تُدْرِكُهُ ٱلْأَبْصَٰرُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ ٱلْأَبْصَٰرَ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ

"Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives [all] vision; and He is the Subtle, the Acquainted." (Quran 6:103)

And while everything in creation is passing, God alone remains.

كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍۢ

"Everyone upon the earth will perish," (Quran 55:26)

وَيَبْقَىٰ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو ٱلْجَلَٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ

"And there will remain the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor." (Quran 55:27)

That is the Quranic portrait: one God, uncreated and unending, supremely merciful and perfectly just, intimately aware of every creature, and absolutely beyond comparison with anything He has made.

sections.title: God in the Bible
sections.body:
The Bible's portrait of God begins with His self-identification to Moses at the burning bush: 'God said to Moses, "I AM WHO I AM," and he said, "You shall tell the children of Israel this: 'I AM has sent me to you.'"' (Exodus 3:14). God is the One who simply is — self-existent, depending on nothing. Readers may notice how close this stands to the Quran's al-Samad, the Eternal Refuge on whom all things depend.

The foundational creed of the Hebrew Bible is the Shema: 'Hear, Israel: Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one.' (Deuteronomy 6:4). Yahweh is the personal name of God in the Hebrew text; the World English Bible renders it directly. This verse remains the heartbeat of Jewish faith, and Christians affirm it too.

God's eternity is sung in the Psalms: 'Before the mountains were born, before you had formed the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, you are God.' (Psalms 90:2). The prophet Isaiah adds His inexhaustible power and knowledge: 'Haven't you known? Haven't you heard? The everlasting God, Yahweh, The Creator of the ends of the earth, doesn't faint. He isn't weary. His understanding is unsearchable.' (Isaiah 40:28).

What is this eternal Creator like in character? The Hebrew Bible's fullest answer comes when God proclaims His own attributes to Moses: 'Yahweh passed by before him, and proclaimed, "Yahweh! Yahweh, a merciful and gracious God, slow to anger, and abundant in loving kindness and truth, keeping loving kindness for thousands, forgiving iniquity and disobedience and sin; and who will by no means clear the guilty, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children, and on the children's children, on the third and on the fourth generation."' (Exodus 34:6-7). Mercy comes first and overflows — yet justice is not cancelled. Anyone who knows the Quran's pairing of al-Rahman al-Rahim with al-'Aziz (the Exalted in Might) will recognize the same balance.

The Hebrew Bible also insists that God is not like His creatures: 'God is not a man, that he should lie, nor a son of man, that he should repent. Has he said, and will he not do it? Or has he spoken, and will he not make it good?' (Numbers 23:19). And in the New Testament, Jesus himself teaches that 'God is spirit, and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth.' (John 4:24) — God is not physical, not confined to a place or a form.

Alongside all this, the New Testament speaks of God as 'Father' and of Jesus as God's 'Son,' language that mainstream Christian theology developed into the doctrine of the Trinity. On the Christian account — and fairness requires stating it as Christians state it — this is still monotheism: one divine being, one God, existing eternally as three persons. 'Begotten,' in the classical creeds, is explicitly said to mean an eternal relationship within God, not that God physically fathered a child.

sections.title: Side by Side: Agreement and Difference
sections.body:
Start with the common ground, because it is enormous. Both scriptures declare God's oneness: 'Say, "He is Allah, [who is] One' (Quran 112:1) and 'Hear, Israel: Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one.' (Deuteronomy 6:4). Both declare His eternity: 'from everlasting to everlasting, you are God' (Psalms 90:2) alongside 'Everyone upon the earth will perish, And there will remain the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor' (Quran 55:26-27). Both name Him the all-knowing Creator: 'The Creator of the ends of the earth... His understanding is unsearchable' (Isaiah 40:28) and 'the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner... the Exalted in Might, the Wise' (Quran 59:24). Both put mercy at the front of His self-description — 'a merciful and gracious God, slow to anger' (Exodus 34:6) and 'the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful' (Quran 59:22) — while both also affirm that He 'will by no means clear the guilty' (Exodus 34:7) and 'will be recompensed' every deed (Quran 7:180).

Now the differences, stated plainly. Islam holds that God's oneness is absolute and indivisible: no persons within God, no incarnation (God taking on a human body and nature), no son in any sense. Mainstream Christianity holds that the one God exists eternally as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, and that the Son became human in Jesus of Nazareth — one God, three persons, as Christians themselves define their monotheism.

The texts say the following. The Quran states that God 'neither begets nor is born' and has no 'equivalent' (Quran 112:3-4), and that 'Vision perceives Him not' (Quran 6:103). The Hebrew Bible states that 'God is not a man, that he should lie, nor a son of man' (Numbers 23:19) and Jesus states that 'God is spirit' (John 4:24). Christians respond that the incarnation does not make God 'a man' by nature — rather, the eternal Son assumed a human nature in addition to his divine nature, and 'begotten' in the creeds means an eternal, non-physical relationship. That is a genuine and sophisticated position, and it deserves to be engaged rather than mocked.

The Islamic engagement is this: every verse quoted above — from both books — describes God without triunity, incarnation, or divine sonship. The Shema needs no footnote. Exodus 34's portrait of mercy and justice is complete without a cross. Numbers 23:19 draws the line between God and man exactly where the Quran draws it. The Trinity is a later interpretive framework laid over these texts; the Quran's description requires no such framework, and on the plain sense of the shared scriptures, the simpler description is the one both books state outright.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
'Allah is a different god from the God of the Bible.' The word 'Allah' is simply the Arabic word for God — the same word Arabic-speaking Christians use in their Bibles and church services today, and a close cousin of the Hebrew words for God (Eloah, Elohim). The Quran explicitly identifies the God it proclaims with the God of Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. Whether Muslims and Christians describe that God correctly is the real debate; which deity they intend to worship is not.

'Christians worship three gods.' Mainstream Christianity has always rejected this. The doctrine of the Trinity claims one God in three persons, and Christian theologians call belief in three gods (tritheism) a heresy. Islam's critique is not that Christians knowingly worship three gods; it is that the Trinity compromises pure monotheism in effect, even though Christians intend monotheism.

'The God of the Quran is distant and unloving, while the God of the Bible is intimate.' The verses quoted above resist this. The Quran's most repeated divine names are 'the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful' (Quran 59:22), and it commands believers to call on God directly by His beautiful names (Quran 7:180) — no priest, no intermediary. Conversely, 'The God of the Bible is all wrath' fails too: Exodus 34:6-7 leads with mercy, grace, and abundant loving kindness before it mentions justice. Both books present a God who is both tender and just.

'The Quran denies that God has any attributes humans can relate to.' Not so. The Quran denies that God resembles creatures ('Nor is there to Him any equivalent,' Quran 112:4), but it fills its pages with relational names: the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer (Quran 59:23). Transcendence and closeness are affirmed together: vision cannot grasp Him, yet He is 'the Subtle, the Acquainted' (Quran 6:103) — aware of every detail of every life.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Set the two portraits side by side one last time. One God, eternal, self-existent, Creator of everything, all-knowing, merciful before all else yet never unjust, unlike anything He made. Up to this point the Quran and the Bible speak with one voice — and Islam says that this shared voice is the original message, taught by every prophet from Abraham to Moses to Jesus.

Where the two traditions diverge, the Islamic case rests on the texts themselves. 'He neither begets nor is born, nor is there to Him any equivalent' (Quran 112:3-4) does not contradict the Hebrew Bible; it echoes its plainest statements — 'Yahweh is one' (Deuteronomy 6:4), 'God is not a man... nor a son of man' (Numbers 23:19), 'God is spirit' (John 4:24). The Quran asks the reader to take those statements at face value, without the interpretive scaffolding of councils and creeds that came generations after Jesus.

This is why Islam describes itself not as a rival to the faith of Moses and Jesus but as its continuation and purification. Tawhid — the absolute oneness of God — is presented as the belief every prophet preached and every scripture, read plainly, still contains. The invitation of the Quran is correspondingly simple: return to worshiping the One whom Abraham worshiped, with nothing and no one alongside Him, calling on Him directly by His most beautiful names. He is, in the words both traditions can read together, the Everlasting Creator who does not faint or grow weary — and, in the Quran's closing image, when everything else has passed away, 'there will remain the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor' (Quran 55:27).

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims and Christians worship the same God?
A: Both communities intend to worship the God of Abraham — one eternal Creator. The word 'Allah' is simply Arabic for God and is used by Arabic-speaking Christians too. The disagreement is over God's nature (Trinity and incarnation versus absolute oneness), not over which Creator is meant.

Q: Does the Bible ever call God 'one' the way the Quran does?
A: Yes. Deuteronomy 6:4 — 'Hear, Israel: Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one.' — is the central creed of the Hebrew Bible, and Jesus and his earliest followers affirmed it. The Quran's Surah Al-Ikhlaas makes the same declaration with added precision: no begetting, no being begotten, no equivalent.

Q: Is the God of the Quran merciful, or mainly a judge?
A: The Quran's most frequent divine names are 'the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful' (Quran 59:22), recited by Muslims before nearly every daily act. Justice is equally real — deeds are recompensed — but Islamic teaching, like Exodus 34:6-7, places mercy first.

Q: Why does Islam reject calling God 'Father'?
A: The Quran avoids parent-child language for God because, in its view, such language historically slid into literal sonship and division within God. It states that God 'neither begets nor is born' (Quran 112:3). God's tenderness is expressed instead through names like the Entirely Merciful, the Subtle, the Acquainted.

Q: Can anyone see God, according to these scriptures?
A: The Quran says 'Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives [all] vision' (Quran 6:103) — God cannot be seen in this life, though Muslims believe the people of Paradise will see Him in the hereafter. The Bible teaches that 'God is spirit' (John 4:24), while mainstream Christianity holds that God made Himself visible in Jesus — one of the key points where the two faiths differ.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below are offered as general further reading for readers who want to go deeper. They are listed as starting points, not as sources for specific claims in this article; every scriptural quotation above comes directly from the cited translations (Saheeh International via api.alquran.cloud, and the World English Bible via bible-api.com).

Al-Ghazali — The Ninety-Nine Beautiful Names of God (al-Maqsad al-Asna): a classical Muslim exploration of the divine names quoted in Quran 7:180 and 59:22-24.

Tim Winter (ed.) — The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology: accessible academic essays on how Muslim scholars understood God's oneness and attributes.

Alister E. McGrath — Christian Theology: An Introduction: a standard, widely used textbook presenting the Trinity and incarnation as mainstream Christian scholarship understands them.

Karen Armstrong — A History of God: a readable comparative history of how Jews, Christians, and Muslims have understood the one God.

Gai Eaton — Islam and the Destiny of Man: a reflective introduction to the Islamic vision of God and humanity, written for Western readers.

title: How This Library Labels Its Sources
subtitle: What Draft, Under Review, Published, Source Pending, and Verified mean — and why nothing goes live until every citation is checked
summary: Every article and every quotation in this library carries a visible status label. Articles move through Draft, Under Review, and Published, while individual citations and scripture quotations are marked Source Pending until they have been checked word-for-word against a named original, at which point they become Verified. No article is published until every citation inside it is verified. This page explains each label, why the system exists, and how you can report an error if you find one.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Every article in this library carries a status label — Draft, Under Review, or Published — and every quotation inside it carries its own label: Source Pending until it has been checked word-for-word against a named original, then Verified. One hard rule connects them: no article is published until every citation in it is verified.

If you spot a mistake, you can report it; a confirmed error sends the article back into review.

sections.title: Why a Trust System at All?
sections.body:
Anyone who has read religious debates online knows the problem: verses with wrong references, scholars quoted saying things they never said, claims copied from page to page until they feel true. A bad argument for the truth still damages trust.

This library exists for sincere seekers weighing questions that may shape the rest of their lives. Such readers deserve better than 'trust us.' So we label the checking status of every article and every citation — and we keep the labels honest even when the honest answer is 'not fully checked yet.'

sections.title: What Do the Labels Mean?
sections.body:
There are two families of labels: one for whole articles, one for individual citations.

Articles move through three stages. Draft means the article is written but not yet checked; it is not shown to general readers. Under Review means checking is underway: quotations are compared against their originals, historical claims against reference works, and the framing of both religions' positions is read for fairness. Published means the article passed the full review and is visible in the library.

Citations have a two-stage life. Source Pending means a quotation has been entered but not yet checked. Verified means it was compared word-for-word against a named source: the Tanzil Uthmani text with the Saheeh International translation for the Quran, the World English Bible for the Bible, and the original page for scholarly quotations. A citation that cannot be traced to its original is removed.

One hard rule connects the two systems: an article cannot be Published while any citation is still Source Pending. No exceptions.

Readers complete the system. Every published article carries an error-report link, and a confirmed error — a misquoted verse, a wrong date, an unfair summary of a Christian position — returns the article to Under Review until it is fixed.

sections.title: An Old Standard, Not a New Gimmick
sections.body:
The instinct behind these labels is ancient, and especially deep in Islamic scholarship. To separate reliable reports (hadith) of the Prophet Muhammad's words and actions (peace be upon him) from unreliable ones, Muslim scholars demanded an isnad — a stated chain of the people who transmitted each report — studied those transmitters' memory and honesty, and openly graded reports from sound to fabricated.

Christian scholarship built its own discipline, textual criticism: gathering the Bible's hand-copied manuscripts, comparing their differences, and openly printing variant readings in modern critical editions.

These labels are a small, modern echo of both traditions: state where every claim comes from, and let the grade be public.

sections.title: What the Quran Says About Verifying Information
sections.body:
The Quran addresses the handling of information directly. In Surah Al-Hujuraat, God commands:

يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن جَآءَكُمْ فَاسِقٌۢ بِنَبَإٍۢ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ أَن تُصِيبُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بِجَهَٰلَةٍۢ فَتُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلْتُمْ نَٰدِمِينَ

'O you who have believed, if there comes to you a disobedient one with information, investigate, lest you harm a people out of ignorance and become, over what you have done, regretful.' (Quran 49:6, Saheeh International)

The key Arabic command is fatabayyanu — investigate, make things clear before acting. This verse is the operating rule behind the Source Pending label: a quotation stays unpublished until verification is complete.

A second verse widens the principle. In Surah Al-Israa, God says:

وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْبَصَرَ وَٱلْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُو۟لَٰٓئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْـُٔولًۭا

'And do not pursue that of which you have no knowledge. Indeed, the hearing, the sight and the heart - about all those [one] will be questioned.' (Quran 17:36, Saheeh International)

Repeating an unchecked claim is not neutral; a person is accountable before God for what they pass along. So the labels err on the side of caution: better for a true quotation to wait than for a false one to travel under our name.

citations.title: The Quran — Tanzil Uthmani text with Saheeh International translation (served via api.alquran.cloud)
citations.note: Arabic text and translation fetched programmatically per verse; verify spot-checks against a printed mushaf and the published Saheeh International edition before final publication.

citations.title: The Holy Bible, World English Bible (public domain)
citations.note: Verse text fetched programmatically via bible-api.com; public-domain translation suitable for quotation.

sections.title: A Standard Christians Recognize Too
sections.body:
This article needs no Bible quotations, but the instinct to verify is one Christians recognize from their own scripture. Paul instructs the church in Thessalonica:

'Test all things, and hold firmly that which is good.' (1 Thessalonians 5:21, World English Bible)

We quote it as an invitation: test this library by its own standard. Every Bible verse here is drawn verbatim from the World English Bible, a modern public-domain translation — never paraphrased from memory, never trimmed to change its sense, never marked Verified until checked against that named edition. If a Christian reader ever finds their scripture misquoted here, the error-report link exists precisely for them.

citations.title: The Quran — Tanzil Uthmani text with Saheeh International translation (served via api.alquran.cloud)
citations.note: Arabic text and translation fetched programmatically per verse; verify spot-checks against a printed mushaf and the published Saheeh International edition before final publication.

citations.title: The Holy Bible, World English Bible (public domain)
citations.note: Verse text fetched programmatically via bible-api.com; public-domain translation suitable for quotation.

sections.title: One Standard for Both Religions
sections.body:
A trust system is only trustworthy if it is symmetrical, so the labels apply identically to Islamic and Christian material. A Quran verse and a Bible verse pass through the same pipeline; a claim about early church history meets the same evidentiary bar as one about early Islamic history; an untraceable quotation is removed whether it comes from a Christian scholar or a Muslim one.

The same symmetry governs descriptions: before any comparison is drawn, each religion's position is presented the way its own mainstream scholarship states it. An article that summarized the Trinity in words no informed Christian would accept could not survive review, any more than one misstating Tawhid (the Islamic doctrine of God's absolute oneness).

This is not neutrality about truth — this library argues openly for Islam. But a true position gains nothing from weakened opposition: Islam's case is strongest when the Christian case is presented at full strength and every quotation on both sides is exact.

sections.title: What the Labels Do Not Mean
sections.body:
'Draft means the article is wrong.' No — it means checking has not finished, so the label does not yet ask for your trust.

'Verified means the library endorses the source.' No — Verified is a claim about accurate quotation, not agreement. A Bible verse marked Verified matches the World English Bible exactly; it does not mean we accept every doctrine built on it.

'Published means final.' No — a confirmed error can send a published article back to Under Review. The labels are a live status, not an unbreakable seal.

'The labels are just branding.' That claim is testable: pick any quotation in a published article and check it against the named source; if the words are not there, the error report works.

sections.title: Verification as an Act of Faith
sections.body:
For Muslims, careful sourcing is not merely good editorial practice; it is obedience. The Quran commands believers to 'investigate' information before acting on it (Quran 49:6) and warns that a person 'will be questioned' about what they repeat (Quran 17:36). A library built to present Islam's case is therefore bound, by Islam itself, to the strictest accuracy — especially when quoting the scriptures of others.

There is a quiet argument inside the system too: a position that fears checking hides its sources, while a position confident of the truth invites scrutiny.

So read published articles knowing every citation has been verified, treat Draft and Source Pending with the caution they ask for, and if you find an error, report it. Correcting us is not an attack; it is a service to the truth we are all accountable for.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: What is the difference between Draft and Under Review?
A: Draft means checking has not finished and the article is not shown to general readers; Under Review means checking is actively underway.

Q: What does Verified mean for a scripture quotation?
A: It was compared word-for-word against a named edition — a claim about accurate quotation, not endorsement of any interpretation.

Q: Can a Published article lose its status?
A: Yes. A confirmed error rolls it back to Under Review until fixed.

Q: How do I report an error?
A: Use the error-report link on any published article; describe the problem and, if you can, point to the correct source.

Q: Why does one unverified citation hold up a whole article?
A: Because a single false quotation can mislead a seeker, and the Quran commands verification before information is acted on (Quran 49:6).

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body: The verification instinct behind these labels has deep roots in both traditions. These works introduce how Muslim scholars authenticated reports about the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and how Christian scholars study the Bible's transmission. None is quoted in this article; they are starting points for the larger story.
